Introduction
Over the past several decades, the prevalence of chronic disease in 
US adults has continued to rise. 256 According to data from the 
2018 National Health Interview Survey, over half the adult US 
population is living with at least one of ten chronic conditions, 
24.6% are living with one chronic health condition, and 27.2% 
are living with two or more chronic conditions. 33 These figures 
likely underestimate the true number of US adults living with 
chronic disease because an additional 6.9% of US adults reported 
ever having long COVID, and 3.4% had long COVID in 2022.5 
While 13% of the total adult population live in the community 
with a disability, 257 the rate of disability is higher in rural areas 
(14.7%) compared to urban (12.6%) and significantly increases 
with age rising to 46% of people over age 75 years. As the  
American and global population ages, there is an increasing num-
ber of people living with chronic medical conditions and disabil -
ity, often with multiple comorbidities and functional limitations 
impacting quality of life. The physical medicine and rehabilitation 
(PM&R) physician or physiatrist and the rehabilitation medicine 
• When prescribing a rehabilitation program for potential kidney 
transplant patients (preoperative or postoperative), one must 
consider their markedly impaired cardiorespiratory and 
musculoskeletal status.
• Kidney transplant candidates are uniquely suited for 
rehabilitation because they experience a profound loss of 
functional capacity while waiting years on dialysis for a donor 
kidney; hence, exercise training in kidney transplantation 
recipients results in significantly improved exercise tolerability.
• In the immediate and early stages following heart or lung 
transplant, the physiatrist plays a crucial role in identifying and 
managing functional deficits to optimize recovery and 
transition transplant recipients to the most appropriate 
outpatient rehabilitation programs.
• Outpatient cardiac rehabilitation is essential in optimizing the 
altered cardiac physiology noted following heart 
transplantation, including resting tachycardia, a delayed rise in 
heart rate in response to exercise, and marked physiologic 
deconditioning as evidenced by a significantly reduced 
maximal oxygen consumption.
• Exercise is a key component in the prevention and 
management of diabetes mellitus, and physiatrists are ideally 
trained to prescribe rehabilitation programs to manage 
associated diabetic complications.
29
Chronic Medical Conditions:  
Pulmonary Disease, Organ 
Transplantation, and Diabetes
JONATHAN H. WHITESON, JEFFREY COHEN, AND SOFIYA PRILIK
• As the US population ages, an increasing number of individuals 
are living with chronic disabling medical conditions requiring 
the perspective and skill set of a physiatrist and rehabilitation 
team.
• Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is the leading cause of 
chronic disabling lung disease and the third leading cause of 
death globally.
• Pulmonary rehabilitation is indicated for individuals physically 
and functionally limited by chronic lung diseases and 
encompasses exercise (aerobic, strength, posture, breathing), 
education, nutrition, and supportive therapies.
• Pulmonary rehabilitation should be delivered through the 
continuum of care from the intensive care unit, in the acute 
medical units, in inpatient rehabilitation and skilled nursing 
facilities, and in the outpatient and community setting.
• Pulmonary rehabilitation before and after lung transplant 
addresses the pulmonary, musculoskeletal, and neurologic 
functional deficits, which is integral to the success of lung 
transplantation, an increasingly viable and utilized surgical 
treatment for individuals with chronic lung diseases.
• The use of prehabilitation and the introduction of an intensive 
early rehabilitation program for liver transplant recipients has 
been found to be well tolerated and feasible in the intensive 
care unit.
SUMMARY PEARLS

SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
Facts Regarding Chronic Obstructive 
Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
• The majority of patients with COPD have asthma.
• Asthma is the most common childhood chronic disease.
• Causes of COPD are asthma, chronic bronchitis, and emphysema, singly or 
in combination.
• Smoking is the primary risk factor for COPD. Approximately 80% of COPD 
deaths are caused by smoking.
• Alpha 1-antitrypsin deficiency is underrecognized by physicians.
• COPD is the third leading cause of death worldwide.
• In 2019, there were 3.23 million deaths worldwide from COPD.
• In 2021, in the United States, .72,000 females died compared to  
.66,000 males.
• In 2020, the cost to the United States for COPD was $49 billion and  
projected to rise to $60.5 billion by 2029.
• BOX 29.1
team play an ever-increasing role in the continuum of care in our 
evolving health care system in the management of such patients. 
Individuals with chronic pulmonary and cardiovascular complica-
tions, diabetes mellitus, renal and hepatic diseases, and those  
undergoing solid organ transplants (SOTs) require rehabilitative 
services and the expertise of a PM&R physician. It is essential that 
the rehabilitation medicine specialist be knowledgeable in the 
management of such conditions and potential complications.
Pulmonary Rehabilitation
Definition
Pulmonary rehabilitation (PR) is defined as an evidence-based, 
multidisciplinary, comprehensive intervention that can be inte -
grated into the management of individuals with chronic lung 
disease across the continuum of care. 227 PR is a multifaceted 
program, which involves optimization of medical management, 
physical conditioning, education, nutritional counseling, coping 
skills, and psychosocial support with the goal of reducing symp -
toms, increasing strength exercise tolerance, improving functional 
status, promoting psychological well-being and quality of life, and 
reducing health care resource use.230
Classification of Pulmonary Diseases
Pulmonary diseases can be classified into four major categories: 
obstructive lung disease, restrictive lung disease, pulmonary vascu-
lature disease, and hypoventilation syndromes. Obstructive lung 
diseases, the most common diagnostic presentation for PR, are 
characterized by expiratory airflow limitation on pulmonary func-
tion testing (PFT) and include chronic obstructive pulmonary 
disease (COPD), chronic bronchitis and emphysema, bronchiecta-
sis, cystic fibrosis (CF), and asthma. Expiratory airflow limitation 
results from a decrease of the diameter of the bronchial lumen due 
to mucous blockage, inflammation and edema of the bronchial 
walls, or dynamic expiratory collapse due to loss of supportive 
structure. Restrictive lung diseases, characterized by a reduction in 
all lung volumes on PFT , are classified as intrinsic with lung paren-
chymal involvement, as in interstitial lung diseases, or extrinsic to 
the lung, as in obesity and neuromuscular disorders. These condi-
tions cause a physical reduction in lung size/volume or increased 
lung stiffness, resulting in a decreased volume of inspired air. Dis-
orders of the pulmonary vasculature include pulmonary embolism 
(PE), pulmonary hypertension (PH), and pulmonary venoocclusive 
disease. Hypoventilation syndromes include obstructive sleep apnea 
and obesity hypoventilation syndrome. Many respiratory diseases 
fit into one of these categories, but individuals may present with a 
combination or mixed pattern of disease. The most common reason 
for referral to PR is COPD.
Differentiating Chronic Bronchitis  
and Emphysema
Chronic bronchitis is a clinical diagnosis based on the volume of 
daily mucous production over months and years cleared by cough 
and typified by acute exacerbations, often infective, and relatively 
quiescent remissions. Excess mucus, resulting from an increased 
number and size of mucus-secreting goblet cells stimulated by 
elements in tobacco smoke and by infection, blocks airways caus-
ing obstruction. Mucous plugging leads to ventilation/perfusion 
(V/Q) mismatching and can progress to significant hypoxemia, 
marked hypercapnia, PH, and right heart failure (i.e., cor pulmo-
nale, the classic blue bloater , with dyspneic, cyanotic, and  
volume overload). Airway inflammation and bronchospasm con -
tribute to airflow obstruction. Emphysema is a pathologic diagno-
sis based on evidence of airway destruction distal to the terminal 
bronchiole on high-resolution lung computed tomography (CT) 
scan or lung biopsy (now rarely done). Elements in tobacco 
smoke activate proteases that cause destruction of elastins and 
supportive tissues in the lung interstitium leaving bronchial walls 
unsupported and prone to collapse on expiration resulting in 
airway obstruction. There is no V/Q mismatching due to equal 
destruction of respiratory epithelium and vasculature resulting in 
significant hypoxemia, mild hypercapnia, and air trapping (the 
classic pink puffer , with dyspneic, noncyanotic, and barrel chest).
Epidemiology of Lung Disease
COPD, the most common form of lung disease and the third 
leading cause of chronic morbidity and mortality in the United 
States and worldwide, is primarily attributable to smoking. 51,159
Approximately 25% of adults with COPD have never smoked, 
and workplace exposures likely contributed to their disease. 242 
Worldwide, other significant contributors include fossil-fuel 
burning furnaces (for home heating and cooking) and environ -
mental pollution or smog. Encouragingly, current smoking rates 
among US adults has declined from 20.9% in 2005 to 14% in 
2017 to 11.5% in 2021, according to the US Centers for Disease 
Control and Prevention (CDC). 65 However, each day in the 
United States, about 3200 youth under 18 years of age smoke 
their first cigarette, and more than 400 youth under 18 years of 
age become daily cigarette smokers. 240 Cigarette smoking  
accounts for more than 480,000 deaths every year, or about one 
in five deaths. Approximately 80% of COPD deaths are caused by 
smoking. According to the CDC, in 2021, there were 14.2 million 
American adults (6.5% of the adult population) currently diag -
nosed with COPD, but an estimated 12 million more were living 
with undiagnosed COPD.154 COPD facts and statistics are shown 
in Box 29.1.167,221
Restrictive pulmonary disease is most commonly caused by the 
following:
• Interstitial lung diseases: conditions that cause pulmonary 
fibrosis (autoimmune related: scleroderma, rheumatoid  
arthritis; inorganic dust exposure: asbestosis, silicosis;  
idiopathic: pulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis)

• Neuromuscular disorders (e.g., amyotrophic lateral sclero -
sis, spinal cord injury [SCI], muscular dystrophy)
• Lung/thoracic volume reduction (e.g., pleural disorders: 
mesothelioma and effusion, lung resection; abnormalities 
of the thoracic spine: scoliosis/kyphosis; abnormalities of 
the chest wall: pectus excavatum)
In SCI, lesions above C3 paralyze the diaphragm (C3–C5) as 
well as the intercostal muscles of respiration, and patients will 
require mechanical ventilation. Injury below C5 leaves the  
diaphragm intact, and respiratory impairment depends on the 
level of injury and extent of intercostal weakness. Below T6, no 
significant inspiratory muscle impairment is expected because 
external (and internal) intercostals are innervated by T1 to  
T6 roots. Expiration is typically gravity assisted, but forced expi -
ration (as in coughing) requires force against a closed glottis using 
the abdominal muscles. Lower thoracic lesions will impair ab -
dominal muscle wall control and effective coughing, and rectus 
abdominis and external/internal obliques are innervated by T5 to 
L1 roots. According to the National Spinal Cord Injury Statistical 
Center, as of 2018, there were an estimated 255,000 to 383,000 
people with SCI or spinal cord dysfunction living in the United 
States.144 Of these patients, 59.7% had tetraplegia (incomplete 
47.1% or complete 12.5%) with significant involvement of the 
muscles of respiration. Duchenne muscular dystrophy is one of 
the more common neuromuscular diseases that cause restrictive 
pulmonary dysfunction, with an incidence of 1 per 3500 male 
infants in the United States.163
Acute PE ranks third among the most common types of car -
diovascular diseases (CVDs), with an annual incidence estimated 
to be 60 to 70 per 100,000. Untreated, the mortality rate is 30%, 
dropping to 8% if appropriately treated. Risk factors include age, 
personal history of deep vein thrombosis or PE, active malig -
nancy, disabling conditions such as heart or respiratory failure, 
coagulation disorders, hormone replacement therapy (HRT), and 
oral contraception.26
Primary, or idiopathic, PH has no clear etiology but is likely 
related to genetic susceptibility. Approximately half the cases of 
PH occur in association with other diseases, such as connective 
tissue disease, heart disease (including congenital heart disease), 
coronary artery disease, systemic hypertension, liver disease  
(cirrhosis), and lung diseases (including blood clots to the lungs 
and emphysema). The exact incidence of PH is not known, but 
about 200,000 related hospitalizations occur annually in the 
United States. The incidence increases with age and is more  
common among females and non-Hispanic Blacks. PH is associ -
ated with hypoxia and right heart failure. Appropriate oxygen 
supplementation in patients with COPD can prevent or reverse 
PH and is part of the management of cor pulmonale. 191
Between 5% and 10% of the US adult population have diag -
nosed obstructive sleep apnea, 7% have breathing pauses during 
sleep that put them at risk for more severe sleep events, and 23% to 
59% snore. Sleep-disordered breathing is more likely to be reported 
by males than females. However, over 80% with signs and  
symptoms of moderate to severe sleep-disordered breathing remain 
undiagnosed. Sleep-disordered breathing is associated with in -
creased risk for coronary artery disease, heart failure, and stroke.223 
As a novel cause of pulmonary disease and disability, acute infection 
with SARS-CoV-2 (the virus that causes COVID-19) is noted  
to result in acute injury to the lung parenchyma, large and small 
airways, and pulmonary vasculature. While most with acute lung 
injury recover with no lasting symptoms or pathologic findings, 
some go on to develop lung fibrosis and progressive respiratory 
symptoms and functional limitations. 126 Approximately 7% of  
patients hospitalized with COVID-19 have been noted to have 
persistent lung radiologic abnormalities for 12 months postinfec -
tion.237 While this pathologic process may be part of the symptom 
complex of postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2, or long COVID, 
many with mild acute COVID-19 and persistent respiratory  
symptoms, including shortness of breath at rest and/or on exertion, 
have normal pulmonary radiologic, vascular, and function testing.  
A number of theories have been put forward to account for this, 
including hyperventilation, cardiac dysfunction, physiologic decon-
ditioning, and anxiety.273 Although the exact etiologic mechanism(s) 
has not been elucidated to date, it is likely to be multifactorial.
Symptoms of Pulmonary Disease
Typical presenting symptoms of pulmonary disease include a  
history of worsening shortness of breath on progressively less  
intense exertion, cough with or without sputum production, pro-
gressive weakness, and fatigue. Functional decline is evidenced by 
limitations with activities of daily living (ADLs), balance disorder 
and falls, progressive difficulty with steps, and limitations with 
community ambulation. Often patients present late with COPD 
due to a progressive accommodation/restriction in lifestyle to 
avoid symptoms. As such, social isolation and depression are often 
present. Shortness of breath is multifactorial, and it is important 
to rule out other significant causes, including cardiac disease. 
Anxiety and cognitive decline are also common. Quality of life is 
universally impacted in later, more advanced stages of chronic 
pulmonary diseases. It is worth stating that many people with 
PFT or radiologic changes consistent with early chronic obstruc -
tive or restrictive lung disorders will be asymptomatic. However, 
treatment should not be delayed until symptoms develop because 
prompt management can impact the course of disease and main -
tain function.
Treatment Options in Pulmonary Disease
Because the majority of patients referred for PR have COPD, 
treatment options presented here will focus on this disease.  
T reatment involves a multifaceted approach, including optimal 
medical management, oxygen therapy, and PR. With advanced 
pulmonary impairment, other treatment options, including me -
chanical ventilation, can be considered. Surgical options, includ -
ing partial lung resection (lung volume reduction surgery [LVRS]) 
and lung transplant, may be helpful. A comprehensive summary 
of treatment options in COPD is found in the Global Initiative 
for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) Pocket Guide to 
COPD Diagnosis.94
General Medical Management
General medical management starts with making/confirming the 
correct diagnosis. A detailed history and physical exam, in combi-
nation with radiologic testing and PFTs, will assist in making an 
accurate diagnosis and establishing the severity of disease. On 
PFT studies, COPD is diagnosed by a value of less than 0.7 for 
the ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) to forced 
vital capacity (FVC). The GOLD classifies the severity of COPD 
based on FEV1 percentage compared to normal (Table 29.1). Life 
expectancy in COPD is strongly correlated with PFTs. The FEV1 
is the best predictor: A 30% mortality rate within 1 year is associ-
ated with an FEV1 less than 750 mL, and 50% within 3 years.

Survival is also negatively impacted by ongoing tobacco  
use, failure to vaccinate as recommended against influenza, poor 
nutritional status, and limited mobility.
Elevated dyspnea rating, although more subjective, has also 
been shown to correlate with poor survival, as have features of cor 
pulmonale and more extensive disease on chest CT .
T obacco/smoking cessation is key for those who continue to 
use tobacco products despite a diagnosis of COPD. Assessing 
readiness to quit is important because motivation and a strong 
commitment to “stick with it” is essential for long-term success. 
A simplified treatment plan encompasses five As:
• Ask if they are smoking and want to quit
• Advise patients to quit, including the benefits of quitting 
and harms of continuing
• Assess readiness and willingness to quit
• Assist in setting a quit date and nicotine replacement 
therapy (NRT) plan
• Arrange follow-up for support
A comprehensive approach includes supportive counseling 
(individual and/or group), behavioral change strategies, centrally 
acting medications (bupropion, varenicline), and NRT . With this 
approach, long-term quit rates are 25% to 40%. Centrally acting 
medications should be prescribed in discussion with other treat -
ing physicians because of side effects, medication interactions, 
and the need for close follow-up. The US Food and Drug Admin-
istration (FDA)–approved NRTs include patches, gum, lozenges, 
inhalers, and nasal spray. Enhanced success is found combining 
daily 24-hour use of the patch and slowly decreasing the dosage 
from 21 mg to 14 mg to 7 mg, then off, with the gum, lozenge, 
inhaler, or nasal spray used as needed.
150 Use of electronic  
cigarettes as an aide to tobacco cessation has been discussed in  
the literature but is not FDA approved at this time.16 Vaccination 
annually for influenza, per protocol for pneumococcal pneumo -
nia, and per current guidelines for COVID-19 and respiratory 
syncytial virus significantly decreases COPD exacerbations and 
associated infections, including pneumonia. Hospitalizations and 
all-cause mortality are similarly reduced.
119,182
Although the PM&R physician may not be the primary 
medication-prescribing physician for patients with COPD, it is 
essential to fully appreciate the spectrum of medications  
available.
42 Understanding of the anatomy and physiology of the 
pulmonary system helps guide pharmacologic therapy. Vagal 
parasympathetic stimulation of cholinergic receptors on bronchial 
smooth muscle cells increases their contraction leading to  
bronchoconstriction. Vagal stimulation also increases goblet cell 
mucous production. Sympathetic activation has the reverse  
effect. Other smooth muscle receptors include beta
2-adrenergic 
stimulation, which relaxes smooth muscle. Cellular receptors  
include histamine and leukotriene. Cellular enzymes include  
cyclic nucleotide phosphodiesterase (PDE), which inactivates  
cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP); cAMP inhibits inflam-
mation and relaxes smooth muscle. Other cells involved in the 
local inflammatory response underlying COPD exacerbations 
include mast cells and lymphocytes.
Bronchodilators include inhaled or nebulized short- or long-
acting (or combined) quaternary anticholinergic (vagolytic) and/
or beta
2-adrenergic agonists (sympathomimetic). Intravenous, 
oral, or inhaled 49 corticosteroids (alone or in combination with 
bronchodilators) decrease the bronchial inflammatory response. 
Mast cell stabilizers and antileukotrienes reverse the allergic  
inflammatory response. Methylxanthines given orally or intrave -
nously support the respiratory effort by increasing the force  
of diaphragm contraction and central respiratory stimulation. 
PDE-4 inhibitors inhibit PDE-4, leading to increased cAMP 
levels and downregulating the inflammatory response. Antibiotic 
therapy for treatment of infectious exacerbations should be 
guided by results of sputum culture and sensitivities. Long-term 
prophylactic antibiotic use may reduce COPD exacerbations. 
Mucolytics (N-acetylcysteine) and expectorants (guaifenesin) help 
to thin and clear tenacious mucous secretions. Intravenous  
alpha
1-antitrypsin infusion may be of benefit in patients with 
deficiency-related emphysema.226 The rehabilitation team plays a 
pivotal role in vaccine and medication compliance, as well as in 
the correct technique and timing of use of a patient’s medical 
regimen. Poor technique and compliance are associated with  
increased COPD exacerbations. In addition, several treatments 
prolong life or reduce progression in this disease,
50 and these  
include smoking cessation, noninvasive mechanical ventilation, 
LVRS, combined long-acting beta agonists with inhaled cortico -
steroids, and oxygen therapy.
Oxygen Therapy
Oxygen therapy reduces mortality when used appropriately in 
patients with COPD and hypoxemia with PO
2 less than  
55 mm Hg or O2sat less than 88%.155,183 Increased life expectancy 
is due to reduction in PH and right heart failure and is directly 
correlated with duration ( .15 hours/day) of use. Other benefits 
include improved strength, reduced fatigue, and improved cogni-
tive function. Oxygen levels may be normal at rest but decrease 
with exertion (tracking oxygen saturations with home/community 
pulse oximetry helps determine the need for portable oxygen  
use with activity). Nocturnal hypoxemia detected by overnight 
oximetry should be managed judiciously with supplemental 
oxygen in patients with documented hypercapnia. Long-term 
oxygen therapy is also needed if the patient’s Sao
2 is less than 89% 
and there is evidence of PH or peripheral edema, suggesting con -
gestive cardiac failure, or polycythemia.196
There is no universally ideal oxygen delivery system. The pros 
and cons need to be considered in the context of each individual 
patient. Wall plug-in oxygen concentrators are the most efficient 
method of providing oxygen in the home; some models provide  
8 to 10 L of continuous nasal cannula flow oxygen. Battery-  
powered portable models (ranging between 6 and 20 pounds)  
are available, but limitations include a relatively short battery life  
(4 to 6 hours), a pulsed/intermittent oxygen flow with lowered  
oxygen delivery (1 to 4 L), and a device weight that can negatively 
impact compliance. Portable concentrators can be used indoors 
with an alternating or direct current inverter. Devices can be

recharged in 2 hours, and backup batteries can be carried. Portable 
concentrators can also be carried on a pull cart. Some companies 
have produced models that provide up to 8 L/min of oxygen and 
have two flow meters so that two individuals can use oxygen from 
the same concentrator if the total use is no more than 8 L/min. 
Other systems incorporate a refill station as part of the concentrator 
so that portable oxygen cylinders can be filled over 2 hours. An 
oxygen-conserving regulator allows the oxygen to last four times as 
long. Oxygen cylinders are a viable alternative to portable concen-
trators and provide continuous flow, but size and weight often 
preclude them from being carried, and they can run out quickly at 
high flow rates. Cylinders can be carried in a push cart or safely 
mounted on a standard or motorized wheelchair if the motors and 
batteries are sealed and both are covered by a rigid housing.
At altitude and when traveling by air, there is reduced partial 
pressure of oxygen, which decreases blood oxygen saturation. Air-
liners are typically pressurized to 8000 to 10,000 feet above sea 
level, and patients with COPD and borderline oxygen saturations 
at ground level may require supplemental oxygen inflight; the need 
is greater for flights over 5 hours. If saturations fall below 92%, 
oxygen should be started. Typically, in those not using supplemen-
tal oxygen at baseline, 1 L is sufficient to maintain adequate O
2sat. 
Otherwise, the rule of thumb is 1 to 2 L over normal flow rates for 
patients on chronic oxygen. Of note, patients cannot take oxygen 
cylinders on planes in the United States, and therefore a portable 
oxygen concentrator is required.
Pulmonary Rehabilitation
PR is the comprehensive approach to optimizing the medical, 
physical, and functional capacity of patients limited by respiratory 
diseases. The PM&R physician must understand medical and 
oxygen management to optimize the PR plan. PR engages a mul-
tidisciplinary team, including chest therapy, physical and occupa-
tional therapy, nutrition, and psychology.
Chest physical therapy
A good understanding of PFTs (Table 29.2 and Fig. 29.1), as well 
as the mechanics and work of breathing in normal and diseased 
states, helps in planning an effective PR program.
102 A normal 
inspiratory to expiratory breathing time ratio is 1:2. Disordered 
breathing patterns with COPD include rapid and shallow ventila-
tion with inadequate expiration (the ratio changes to 1:1 due to 
shortened expiratory time). Air trapping due to dynamic expira -
tory airway collapse contributes to a failure to breathe normal 
volumes of air and results in abnormal gas exchange (a significant 
volume of inspired air oscillates in the respiratory dead space). In 
conjunction with abnormal diaphragmatic excursion, this con -
tributes to an increased work of breathing. Shortness of breath at 
rest and/or on exertion are common complaints of individuals 
with long COVID; etiology of respiratory symptoms in long 
COVID is often multifactorial, and disordered breathing patterns 
have been noted.
71
Although patients are often unaware of their abnormal 
breathing patterns, they often experience respiratory distress and 
air hunger (dyspnea). Breathing exercises under the guidance of 
a physical therapist raise the breathing pattern to a level of 
awareness that when combined with relaxation techniques  
facilitates retraining of that pattern. Retraining techniques for 
persons with COPD include pursed lip breathing, head-  
down and bending-forward postures, slow deep breathing, and 
localized expansion exercises or segmental breathing. These 
techniques maintain positive airway backpressure during exhala -
tion and help reduce airway collapse and hyperinflation.  
Although diaphragmatic breathing is widely taught, it has been 
shown to increase the work of breathing and dyspnea compared 
with the natural pattern of breathing in the patient with 
COPD.
50 Respiratory muscle endurance training focusing on 
inspiratory resistance training is also used to reduce breathing 
fatigue. T raining of the expiratory muscles has also been found 
to be of some value.
173
Airway clearance strategies are indicated for persons with  
(1) abnormal cough mechanics (e.g., muscle weakness), (2) altered 
mucous rheology (e.g., CF), (3) structural airway defects (e.g., 
bronchiectasis), and (4) altered mucociliary clearance (e.g., pri -
mary ciliary dyskinesia).
171
Clearance of secretions is mandatory to reduce the work of 
breathing, improve gas exchange, and limit infection and atelec -
tasis. For secretion clearance through chest physical therapy to  
be effective, mucoactive medications should be given. 214 These 
include expectorants, mucolytics, bronchodilators, surfactants, 
and mucoregulatory agents that reduce the tenacity and volume 
of mucous secretion and support bronchodilation. Use of antitus-
sives for uncontrolled coughing can help limit dynamic airway 
collapse, bronchospasm, or syncope. Cough suppressant use 
should be limited to avoid secretion retention. Use at night to 
support sleep is a consideration.

SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
T echniques for clearing secretions include postural drainage, 
manual or device-induced chest percussion and vibration, device-
induced airway oscillation, incentive spirometry, and other  
devices and measures that improve the ability to cough. Head-
down tilt positions for postural drainage should be u sed with 
caution in persons with severe heart disease.
181
In a manually assisted cough, the patient’s abdomen is com -
pressed while the patient controls the depth of inspiration and the 
timing of closing and opening of the glottis. Noninvasive inter -
mittent positive-pressure ventilation (NIPPV) with air stacking or 
glossopharyngeal breathing (GPB) is used to increase the depth of 
inspiration when inspiratory muscles are too weak to produce a 
deep breath. Air stacking is holding a portion of two or more 
breaths to fully inflate the lungs before exhalation. In GPB, the 
person uses the tongue to breathe. The technique is described 
more fully later in this chapter. When an upper motor neuron 
lesion above the midthoracic level has paralyzed the abdominal 
muscles, functional electrical stimulation of these muscles can 
produce a cough.
116
Breathing techniques to support secretion clearance include 
controlled coughing, huffing (gentle repeated forced expiratory 
flows through a partially closed glottis), active cycle of breathing 
(a three-phase technique), and autogenic drainage (a self-drainage 
technique using different speeds of breathing to move mucus).
Hands-on chest PT includes percussion techniques (tapping, 
cupping, vibration) and postural drainage utilizing gravity and 
positioning to assist drainage from specific lung segments.
Simple respiratory devices that aid secretion clearance include 
an incentive spirometer to promote deep breathing/chest expan -
sion and oscillating positive expiratory pressure devices (Flutter, 
Acapella, Aerobika, and Lung Flute).
Positive expiratory pressure mask therapy followed by huffing 
is a useful technique when other methods of mobilizing secretions

Exercise therapy: general considerations
Aerobic exercise is the goal in any PR program to overcome the 
fatigue from physiologic deconditioning that accompanies chronic 
lung diseases. However, in more advanced chronic lung disease, 
the rehabilitation need may be more basic, including range  
of motion (ROM) exercises, strengthening, transfers, balance, 
posture, and gait training, to get the patient ready for PR. T o be 
appropriate for PR, a patient must demonstrate a decrease in 
functional exercise capacity as a result of pulmonary disease and 
be able to participate safely in a progressive cardiorespiratory  
endurance training program. Patients should be medically stable, 
oriented to the process and goals of the PR program, be motivated 
to exercise on a consistent basis, and ideally be free of tobacco 
products. Following referral for PR, a comprehensive evaluation 
should be completed by the PR team, including a thorough re -
view of the history, a detailed patient examination, and review of 
all diagnostic testing. Ideally, and if available, a pre-participation 
cardiopulmonary exercise test (CPET) quantifies baseline physi -
ologic and functional parameters, assists in risk stratification  
to minimize cardiopulmonary risk of exercise, and provides a basis 
for the initial exercise PR prescription. Indications for CPET have 
been adopted by the American Thoracic Society (ATS) and the 
American College of Chest Physicians (CHEST) (Box 29.2).
13 
Patients who cannot tolerate exercise testing without supplemen -
tal oxygen are not candidates for CPET , and a standard exercise 
stress test (EST) is acceptable. Patients unable to perform stan -
dard ESTs should complete a 6-minute walk test (6MWT).
17
Exercise prescription for pulmonary 
rehabilitation
It is imperative that the personnel supervising the exercise pro-
gram are appropriately trained in the implementation of exercise 
prescriptions in people with chronic illnesses and special needs. 
The American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) offers certifi-
cation programs to those with appropriate backgrounds, such as 
exercise science or kinesiology, nursing, occupational therapy, 
physical therapy, and respiratory therapy. PR exercise prescription 
guidelines are focused on patients with COPD but are appropri -
ate for patients with other pulmonary diseases as well. The 
ATS,
213 CHEST ,211 and ACSM 12 have provided recommenda-
tions for PR exercise. In general, PR exercise programs should 
include endurance training of larger muscle groups. T readmill 
walking is the preferred method because it is generally well toler -
ated, effective for physiologic conditioning, and translates into 
real-world functional improvement. Walking in a hallway with 
assistance of a therapist is acceptable for those too limited to safely 
tolerate the treadmill. Leg and arm cycling, stair stepping, rowing, 
and elliptical exercise machines can also be incorporated. Arm 
exercises can increase ventilatory drive at lower workloads and can 
worsen the patient’s dyspnea during the activity. Resistance/
strength training and flexibility exercises also contribute to func-
tional improvements in patients with pulmonary diseases. The 
most specific exercise prescription guidelines have been provided 
by the ACSM (Table 29.3).
Exertional dyspnea is among the most frequently experienced 
symptoms of pulmonary disease and significantly contributes to 
physical disability and functional impairment. Cardiorespiratory 
exercise training is effective for decreasing exertional dyspnea. 
During a program of progressive aerobic training, cardiovascular, 
respiratory, and neuromuscular system adaptations occur leading 
to an increased ability to sustain physical activity. Following a PR 
program, the efficiency of oxygen delivery (cardiac output) and 
muscle oxygen extraction (arteriovenous oxygen difference), com-
bined with increased mitochondrial oxidative activity, lowers 
blood lactate levels at any given level of strenuous exercise. Lower 
blood lactate lessens carbon dioxide production and the ventila -
tory requirement (or sense of dyspnea) for a given workload.
In conjunction with the PR supervising physician, the initial 
exercise session intensity should be established between patient 
and supervising professional. While patients tend to opt for activ-
ity intensities that minimize fatigue and shortness of breath, it is 
the skill, support, and encouragement of the PR professional that 
enables a patient to push gradually beyond that comfort level to 
an exercise intensity that promotes physiologic change. Close 
monitoring and adjustment of the intensity and duration of the 
session should be made according to the patients’ perception of 
exertion and physiologic responses. The progression of exercise 
intensity and duration over subsequent sessions should reflect the 
individual patient’s functional progress and tolerance of the pro-
gram. Although beneficial results have been observed with PR 
programs of 6 to 10 weeks in some individuals, those with more 
advanced pulmonary disease may require longer periods of par -
ticipation to show significant results. Home exercises should be 
prescribed during the rehabilitation program, and patients are 
encouraged to complete them daily. In anticipation of graduating 
from the monitored PR program, patients should be educated  
in the ongoing need for lifelong exercise. On completion of the 
PR program, the CPET (or standard EST or 6MWT) should be  
repeated to quantify physiologic and functional gains and  
recalibrate the exercise training intensity for the ongoing self-
monitored program.

Exercise in patients with COPD considers the severity of their 
respiratory disability ( Table 29.4).

Exercise in asthma
Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of asthma were 
published in 2007 by the National Heart, Lung, and Blood  
Institute179 and revised in 2020. 1 While exercise is supported in 
the management of asthma, the mainstay of treatment is pharma-
cologic both in prevention of and recovery from exacerbations. 
When asthma is quiescent, often little or no functional limitations 
are seen. Persistent symptoms and progressive functional decline 
can be seen in asthmatic bronchitis or when asthma overlaps with 
COPD. In these settings, benefit is seen from pulmonary rehab as 
for nonasthmatic patients with COPD. Exercise can cause airflow 
obstruction due to exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) or 
as exercise-induced asthma (EIA). 92 Nearly 90% of those with 
asthma experience EIB during exercise. Symptoms typically begin 
during exercise, worsen after completion of exercise, and resolve 
within 1 hour. A late phase of more minor symptoms can be  
seen 4 to 12 hours after stopping exercise and resolving within  
24 hours. Exercise that is most likely to trigger EIA includes exer-
cise in cold or dry weather, especially outdoor winter sports. 
Sports requiring constant activity, such as long-distance running, 
soccer, and lacrosse, also commonly trigger EIA. Environmental 
triggers include dry and/or cold air, pollution, high pollen or aller-
gen counts, irritants (smoke, fumes), and a recent cold or asthma 
episode.236 EIA is diagnosed with normal pre- and abnormal 
postexercise PFTs indicating EIB. Preventive measures include cov-
ering mouth and nose with a scarf when exercising in cold and dry 
weather, premedicating with bronchodilators before exercising, and 
incorporating a prolonged warmup (10 minutes) before exercise. 
Preferred short-acting bronchodilators include beta 2 agonists  
(albuterol) and anticholinergics (ipratropium). Standard asthma 
control medications should be continued. Inhaled corticosteroid 
inhibit the late-phase inflammatory response.34
T wo major patterns of asthma (intermittent vs. persistent) are 
seen; however, bronchial biopsies in patients with intermittent 
asthma show evidence of ongoing, persistent airway inflamma -
tion. A mouse model of asthma 193 shows exercise decreases the 
activity of a factor that upregulates the expression of genes that 
encode inflammatory mediators. Moderate-intensity aerobic exer-
cise training of the mice decreased inflammation and pathologic 
structural remodeling within the lungs. It is extrapolated that 
aerobic exercise in people with asthma reduces airway inflamma -
tion in a similar manner.
Exercise in CF
The prevalence of CF in 2020 was approximately 40,000 people 
in the United States68 and 70,000 worldwide; it is most prevalent 
in Europe, North America, and Australia. CF is due to an autoso-
mal recessive genetic disorder and significantly impacts life expec-
tancy. The disease is caused by mutation of a gene that encodes a 
chloride-conducting transmembrane channel regulating chloride 
transport and airway mucociliary clearance. This results in mu -
cous retention, chronic airway infection, and local inflammation 
resulting in bronchiectasis, small airway obstruction, and progres-
sive respiratory impairment. CF transmembrane conductance 
regulator dysfunction mainly affects epithelial cells, although 
there is evidence of a role in immune cells. CF affects several body 
systems, and morbidity and mortality are mostly caused by bron-
chiectasis, small airway obstruction, and progressive respiratory 
impairment. Comorbidities include pancreatic malabsorption, 
liver/biliary cirrhosis, impaired sweat gland function and heat 
shock, and infertility due to impaired vas deferens function. 
Quality of life and clinical outcomes have improved in patients 
with CF (median life expectancy is now .40 years) due to the 
development of drugs enhancing the clearance of mucus from  
the lungs and treatment of chronic infection, the correction of 
pancreatic insufficiency, and dietary regimens for malnutrition. 
Small molecule and gene-based therapies have been developed 
that target the basic defect in CF and are effective in improving 
lung function and reducing pulmonary exacerbations. 79,120
PR for CF is a lifelong commitment to medication adherence, 
secretion clearance, nutrition, and exercise. Psychological and 
supportive therapies are essential to embed this complex health 
plan for patients and for family members who are essential par -
ticipants in the overall care plan. Compliance through adolescent 
years is particularly challenging.
The Vest Airway Clearance System (Hill-Rom, St. Paul, MN) 
helps loosen secretions by providing chest wall percussion and 
vibration by rapidly inflating (compressing) and deflating (releas-
ing) the vest on the chest wall, a process called high-frequency 
chest wall oscillation.
Children with CF have reduced anaerobic performance and  
do not participate in activities requiring short bouts of high-  
energy expenditure to the same extent as healthy children. A study 
of anaerobic training on children with CF older than 12 years 
showed no child had an FEV 1 less than 55% of the predicted 
level. Children were trained 2 days per week for 12 weeks, with 
sessions lasting 30 to 45 minutes. Each anaerobic activity lasted 
20 to 30 seconds. 135 Three months after the end of training,  
anaerobic performance and quality of life were significantly 
higher in the trained group. A measurable improvement in aero -
bic performance, however, was not sustained.
The lung function of adults with CF has been studied by  
measuring FEV1. Thirty-six percent were found to have normal  
or mild lung dysfunction (FEV 1, 70% predicted), 39% had  
moderate dysfunction (FEV1, 40% to 69% predicted), and 25% 
had severe dysfunction (FEV 1, ,40% predicted). One-third of 
adults with CF have multiresistance gram-negative organisms. A 
3-year study of regular aerobic exercise in adults with CF has 
found that it reduced the expected decline in pulmonary function 
throughout that period. Appropriate vigorous aerobic exercise 
enhances cardiovascular fitness, increases functional capacity, and 
improves quality of life. Patients with CF should rarely be exer -
cised in close proximity to one another, however, because of the 
possibility of transmission of Burkholderia cepacia, a highly trans-
missible bacterium that frequently infects patients with CF .
Survival in CF is correlated with maximal oxygen uptake. Blau 
et al.32 found that an intensive 4-week summer camp program for 
both children and young adults improved exercise tolerance and 
nutrition in patients with CF .
Exercise in disorders of chest wall function
There are numerous causes of chest wall dysfunction that exist 
with both mechanical and neuromuscular components. The chest 
wall can be viewed as encompassing the rib cage, spine, dia -
phragm, abdomen, shoulder girdles, and neck. Distortions of the 
trunk are also reflected in distortions of the heart, lungs, airway, 
and abdominal contents. Many conditions negatively affect the 
chest wall, including ankylosing spondylitis, pectus excavatum, 
obesity, the sequelae of thoracoplasty or phrenic nerve crush  
for the treatment of pulmonary tuberculosis, neuromuscular dis -
eases with weakness of the respiratory bellows mechanism and

Patients with developmental disorders are a heterogeneous

Nutritional issues
Nutrition evaluation and therapy are essential in patients with 
chronic lung disease presenting for PR. A typical PR program will 
treat patients with low, normal, and high body mass index (BMI) 
representing the spectrum from malnourished/cachectic to obese.
Diet and nutrition are important modifiable risk factors for the 
development, progression, and management of obstructive lung 
diseases such as asthma and COPD through an immunomodula -
tory role of key nutrients. 98 The relationship between dietary 
patterns, nutrient intake, and weight status in obstructive lung 
diseases has been well summarized.28
Dietary patterns improving respiratory diseases include high 
fruit and vegetable intake, Mediterranean-style diet, fish, and 
omega3 intake. Fast-food intake and Westernized highly processed 
dietary patterns have adverse associations. Antioxidants are associ-
ated with positive effects on inflammation, clinical outcomes,  
and respiratory disease prevention, but intervention studies of 
individual antioxidants have not indicated widespread adoption 
of supplementation.263
A whole foods approach to nutrient supplementation with 
increased intake of fruit and vegetables increases intake of multi -
ple nutrients, including vitamins C and E, carotenoids, and flavo-
noids, and shows more potential in reducing COPD 224 and 
asthma exacerbations.274
In moderate to severe COPD, weight loss is common and 
leads to decreased exercise capacity and health status with in -
creased morbidity and mortality. Weight loss is likely the result of 
insulin resistance, elevated catecholamine levels, and dyslipid -
emia. Loss of fat mass is generally caused by energy imbalance, 
whereas loss of fat-free lean/muscle mass results from substrate 
and protein catabolism. 76,216 Nutritional supplementation ther -
apy with daily oral liquid nutritional supplements in nutritionally 
depleted patients with COPD increases body weight, lean muscle 
mass, maximal inspiratory mouth pressure, strength, and peak 
workload. Respiratory symptoms also improve. 67 However, oral 
glucocorticosteroid treatment can blunt this response.
T otal daily energy expenditure in patients with COPD, irre -
spective of their weight, is no different than in healthy people. 244 
Insufficient food/calorie intake is the cause of malnutrition. 
Weight gain is supported when total energy intake is 1.3 times 
higher than the resting energy expenditure.198
Counterintuitively, smaller doses (125 mL) of a dietary supple-
ment promote greater improvements in weight gain (larger volumes 
increase bloating and early satiety, compromise diaphragmatic 
movement, and contribute to meal skipping).39 Nutrition counsel-
ing and promoting self-management for changes in dietary behav-
ior should start early in the course of the disease, when patients with 
COPD are still under the care of a primary physician. 40 Practical 
suggestions for shopping, food preparation, and eating smaller, 
more frequent meals should be offered. When fatigue, dyspnea, 
swallowing, or poor appetite interferes with eating, referral to a  
dietitian is recommended. Involuntary weight loss is best addressed 
preemptively.
Psychosocial support
Biopsychosocial considerations for persons with pulmonary  
dysfunction include the ongoing education of the patient and 
family, vocational counseling, and disability evaluation. Occupa -
tional therapy can also provide patients with severe respiratory 
disease counseling on energy conservation and techniques for 
performing basic ADLs. 158 Depression and anxiety are very  
common comorbidities associated with COPD 192 because of the 
often drastic limitations in functional activities and the distress 
associated with dyspnea. Anxiety and/or depression in patients 
with COPD is associated with increased mortality, COPD exac -
erbation rates, length of hospital stay, and decreased quality of life 
and functional status. T reatment options include psychological 
(e.g., relaxation therapies, cognitive behavioral therapy, self-  
management) and pharmacologic interventions (antidepressant 
and anxiolytic medications). PR improves anxiety and depression, 
but rehabilitation completion rates are negatively affected by their 
presence.202
Summary of recommendations

and grades in the evidence-based guidelines on PR with a strength

Management Options for Individuals  
With Severe Lung Disease and  
Long-Term Outcomes
Supported and Mechanical Ventilation
Self-supported ventilation techniques and mechanically sup -
ported noninvasive ventilation assist regular respiratory efforts 
when patients with chronic lung disorders develop worsening re -
spiratory status either through progression of the chronic disease 
or in an acute exacerbation or infection. Air stacking (adding 
consecutive quantities of air into the lungs), maximal insufflations 
(passively inflating the patient’s lungs to maximal capacity), as -
sisted coughing, and noninvasive ventilation have been detailed 
by Bach,20 who also describes an oximetry feedback respiratory 
aid protocol where the pulse oximeter is used to provide feedback 
of the patient’s oxygen saturation (Spo2). The goal is to maintain 
Spo2 greater than 94%, supported by techniques to maintain  
effective alveolar ventilation and airway secretion elimination. 
With declining Spo2, patients and families are taught to intensify 
airway clearance techniques and use NIPPV continuously. If these 
measures fail to bring the Sp o2 back to acceptable levels, then 
patients must be evaluated by their clinician or in the emergency 
room and admitted if necessary. If admitted, ongoing efforts to 
provide noninvasive assisted ventilation and avoid intubation 
should be made; once intubated, patients with chronic lung dis -
ease (especially neuromuscular disorders) are challenging to extu -
bate and may require tracheotomy and chronic/lifelong invasive  
ventilation.
Noninvasive ventilation (NIPPV) delivered via nasal-mouth 
interfaces is the ventilator method of choice. The equipment is 
lightweight and portable, powered by either alternating or direct

Diaphragmatic pacing (Fig. 29.7), used effectively for over  
40 years, is indicated in respiratory insufficiency with ventilator 
dependency in patients with high-level SCI and central hypoven-
tilation syndromes (Ondine curse). The goal is to wean from 
mechanical ventilation but not to remove the tracheotomy, main-
tenance of which allows mechanical ventilation should the need 
arise due to diaphragmatic pacer failure or any other cause. Dia -
phragmatic pacing is achieved through a surgical procedure to 
place electrodes either directly around the phrenic nerve (intra -
thoracic diaphragm pacing) or directly into the diaphragm muscle 
near the insertion of the phrenic nerve through laparoscopy  
(intraperitoneal diaphragm pacing). Diaphragmatic pacing re -
quires the phrenic nerves to be intact. Diaphragm reconditioning 
is required after implantation. Perioperative mortality is negligible, 
and ventilator weaning rate is reported to be 72% to 96%.  
Although not all patients can be weaned postprocedure, those 
who are experience enhanced quality of life through improved 
patterns of physiologic breathing.
146 The diaphragm pacing system 
consists of an external transmitter and antenna, implanted elec -
trodes, and a receiver. The working life expectancy of the receiver is 
for the lifetime of the patient, and batteries last for 2 to 3 weeks. A 
bipolar electrode is available for use in persons who already have 
demand cardiac pacers. Advanced warning is given of transmitter 
battery failure, with a gradual decrease in tidal volume over several 
days. Although thoracic phrenic nerve implants for infants and 
younger children have required thoracotomy in the past, thoraco-

Lung Volume Reduction in Emphysema
Lung volume reduction (LVR) in patients with advanced  
emphysema improves pulmonary function, exercise capacity, 
and quality of life in a select subset of patients. Patients consid -
ered for LVR include those with advanced emphysema who  
have failed maximal medical therapy, undergone PR, and are  
not smoking. Surgical and bronchoscopic approaches for LVR 
have been described. Careful patient review is essential to deter -
mine approach and select appropriate candidates. Surgical LVR 
surgery has been associated with significant morbidity and mor -
tality, prompting the development of bronchoscopic approaches 
and the deployment of bronchial valves or coils to facilitate 
LVR.207,222
In surgical LVR, 20% to 30% of one or both lungs (usually 
at the apices) is removed resulting in reduced hyperinflation 
(decreased residual volume and total lung capacity) and im -
proved FEV 1 and FVC. 63 The 6MWT increased significantly, 
and quality of life improved. In general, inspiratory muscle 
function (maximal inspiratory pressure and maximal transdia -
phragmatic pressure) also improved. Combining LVRS with PR 
produces greater benefits than exercise alone. 25,110 Not all  
patients will benefit from surgical LVR. Patients with FEV 1 less 
than 20% predicted and homogeneous disease, or a diffusion 
capacity less than 20% predicted, experience an increased  
mortality rate after LVR surgery. Additional studies suggest  
that patients getting the most benefit from LVR surgery are 
those with predominately upper lobe emphysema with low 
postrehabilitation exercise capacity. 180
Conversely, patients with non–upper lobe involvement and 
high postrehabilitation measures of maximal work received little 
benefit from LVR surgery. The results of the National Emphysema 
T reatment T rial suggest that all LVR surgery candidates first enroll 
in a PR program because some patients can improve to the point 
that they might not qualify for the surgery.84
Furthermore, participation in a PR program before LVR 
surgery helps to both identify appropriate surgical candidates 212 
and ensure that candidates have appropriate levels of compli -
ance and an understanding of their condition. 243 Bronchoscopic 
LVR is an alternative treatment option, which is a less invasive 
and potentially beneficial intervention for patients who may not 
be candidates for traditional LVRS. 7 There are many different 
interventions, including various stents, occluders, polymers, 
coils, and thermal vapor ablation. Stapling and plication of  
the most emphysematous tissue are carried out. Atelectasis  
can also be induced in these areas with the use of endobronchial 
sealants or valves. Extraanatomic tracts between the major  
airways and the emphysematous tissue are created and prevented 
from collapsing with stents to facilitate expiratory airflow.
Long-Term Results for Lung Volume Reduction Surgery
The distribution of emphysematous changes in lung parenchyma 
(measured by high-resolution CT) and measures of exercise  
capacity after rehabilitation are important factors in predicting 
the long-term effects of LVR.180 Patients with predominantly up-
per lobe emphysema and lower levels of exercise capacity who 
received LVR surgery continued to have improved survival rates 
after 4 years, improved function after 3 years, and improved  
dyspnea after 5 years. Patients with upper lobe involvement and 
high levels of exercise capacity after rehabilitation continued to 
have symptomatic improvements when assessed after 4 years.  
Patients with non–upper lobe emphysema and low exercise capac-
ity primarily experienced improvements in health-related quality 
of life that became insignificant after 3 years. Patients with non–
upper lobe emphysema and high exercise capacity did not experi-
ence significant benefits from LVR surgery and experienced a 
higher mortality rate during the first 3 years. Importantly,  
improvements in all groups were no longer significant after  
5 years. Overall, LVR surgery in selected patients is effective in 
improving symptoms, function, and quality of life. 112
Bronchoscopic Endobronchial Valve Treatment
A form of bronchoscopic LVR, the placement of one-way endo -
bronchial valves is a viable option for the management of select 
patients with advanced emphysema with severe hyperinflation.136 
Long-term follow-up over 3 years demonstrates sustained  
improvements in PFTs, walking distance, and quality of life.105
Lung Transplantation
As a treatment option for patients with end-stage progressive and 
debilitating lung disease, the rate of lung transplantation (LTx) 
has been steadily increasing since 2000. Since 2016, approxi -
mately 4500 adult lung transplants have been performed globally 
every year. Further growth is attributed to a shortage of lung do -
nors. The most common indications for LTx in adults include 
COPD, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, CF , alpha 1-antitrypsin 
deficiency (AATD), and idiopathic pulmonary arterial hyperten -
sion. CF and primary PH are the two main diseases of childhood 
for which lung transplant is performed. Patients with these diag -
noses need to meet defined criteria to be eligible for transplant.147 
LTx should not be considered in a patient with a florid infection, 
recent malignant tumor (within 2 years), continued addictive be-
havior, smoking, or lack of reliable social support. Relative contra-
indications include older age, poor functional status, obesity, severe 
osteoporosis, and correctable coronary artery disease. 151 Of pro -
grams in North America that have been queried, 58% have a 
minimum requirement for exercise capacity to be considered for 
lung transplant. Covering a minimum of 600 feet on a 6MWT is 
the most common requirement, although some programs require 
only 250 feet. If an individual can only transfer from bed to chair, 
46% of lung transplant centers consider this an absolute contra -
indication to lung transplant, and 52% consider it a relative 
contraindication. Survival in LTx continues to improve in part 
due to an increasing number of bilateral lung transplants (BLTs) 
being performed.269
Long-term results in lung transplant
The median survival for adult lung transplant recipients since 
2010 is 6.7 years and is increasing compared to earlier time 
periods.54 Those undergoing BLTs have a better median survival  
(7.8 years) than single lung recipients (4.8 years). In 2021, 85.3%

Posttransplant survival has improved over time despite the relax -
ation of selection criteria resulting in patients’ characteristics and 
severity at the time of transplant being significantly worse in more 
recent times. One of the main determinants of LTx outcome is 
the underlying disease, with a median survival of 8.9 years for CF 
patients, 6.7 years for COPD with AATD, 5.6 years for COPD 
without AATD, 4.8 years for idiopathic interstitial pneumonia, 
and 2.8 years for retransplantation. Other prognostic factors  
relate to the recipient (sex, age, 6-minute walking distance,  
mechanical ventilation, dialysis, or intensive care unit [ICU] 
stay), the donor (diabetes, age, cause of death), the donor/recipi -
ent interaction (human leukocyte antigen [HLA] mismatches, 
cytomegalovirus [CMV], or sex mismatch), the surgical approach 
(single vs. bilateral), and the center volume. 250 The highest mor-
tality rate occurs during the first year. 254 Sepsis and bronchiolitis 
obliterans remain the two most common causes of death. Cause 
of death following LTx varies with time posttransplantation. 
Thirty-day mortality is related primarily to surgical issues, donor 
lung preservation, and primary graft dysfunction. In the subse -
quent posttransplant period, infectious causes, malignancy,  
and chronic lung allograft dysfunction (CLAD) predominate. 
With significant improvements in short-term outcomes owing to 
improved donor-lung preservation and surgical technique, the 
5-year survival after LTx has increased to nearly 60%, 36 but it 
remains a challenge due to the development of CLAD (i.e., graft 
dysfunction resulting from multiple etiologies, including bronchi-
olitis obliterans of the small airways and fibrosis in the peripheral 
lung tissue). 278 These outcomes should be tempered with the 
knowledge that the mortality rate for those on the transplant 
waitlist is also high, although it is stable at 18.8 deaths per 100 
patient-years.259 Pretransplant mortality rates are higher for males, 
blood group AB, and those with extremes of stature (e.g., tall or 
short). The 3-year survival rate for those in the sickest category  
of the COPD BODE ( BMI, degree of airflow obstruction, func-
tional dyspnea, exercise capacity) index is only 50%.48
Special Considerations of  
Pulmonary Dysfunction
Obesity-Related Pulmonary Dysfunction
The prevalence of obesity (BMI .30) continues to increase, 
reaching 42.4% among adults and 19.3% among youth in the 
United States in 2017-2018. The percentage of US adults aged  
20 years and older who are overweight or obese was 73.1%. 86  
All racial and ethnic groups and both sexes are affected. Primary 
respiratory complications associated with obesity include obstruc-
tive sleep apnea, obesity-hypoventilation syndrome, and asthma.280 
Restrictive pattern on PFTs is seen and may be severe with  
increasing BMI. Chronic hypoxemia related to obesity-hypoven -
tilation syndrome can lead to polycythemia, PH, cardiac dys -
rhythmias, and right ventricular failure. One study reported that 
three of four adults who presented to the emergency department 
with acute asthma were either overweight (BMI 5 25–29.9; 
30%) or obese (BMI .30; 44%).251 Other conditions that can be 
associated with obesity include atelectasis, pneumonia, venous 
thrombosis, and PE.176
Daily physical activity is important in preventing and treating 
overweight and obesity as many of the aforementioned conditions 
are improved with weight loss. Obesity increases the work and 
metabolic requirements of breathing and physical activity, result -
ing in decreased exercise tolerance. The weight relative maximal 
volume of oxygen consumption (VO 2 max) in obese persons can 
be significantly decreased compared with controls. Norman et 
al.184 studied severely obese adolescents (BMI .40) and found 
that although absolute VO 2 max (mL O 2/min) was similar to 
controls, walk/run distance was approximately 60% of the control 
average. Although obese adults with COPD might have greater 
fatigue, functional impairment, and significantly lower 6-minute 
walk distance compared with normal-weight individuals with 
COPD, similar relative improvements in walk distance are seen 
after PR.206
Spinal Cord Injury and Pulmonary Dysfunction
In SCI, there is diminished cardiopulmonary and circulatory 
function as well as lower limb muscle atrophy and bone mass re -
duction. Patients with high cervical cord injuries, including  
children as young as 3 years, can learn neck breathing 93 or GPB 
as a form of voluntary respiration. This allows some time off the 
ventilator. In GPB (or frog breathing), air is pumped into the 
lungs by the patient using the tongue as a piston (Fig. 29.8). Also 
known as glottic press to speech pathologists, it is taught to  
patients who have had a laryngectomy to pump air into the 
esophagus for esophageal speech. GPB can be used for a number 
of purposes in addition to being an alternative form of breathing. 
GPB can improve vocal volume and flow of speech to allow the 
patient to call for help and to provide the deep breath needed for 
an assisted cough.
Future Directions and Areas of  
Opportunity in Pulmonary Dysfunction
Current guidelines recommend outpatient PR after hospitaliza -
tion for a COPD exacerbation. However, recent data suggest  
very poor referral and participation rates with a dismal 1.9% of 
individuals hospitalized for COPD receiving PR within 6 months 
of their hospitalization and just 2.7% within 12 months. 229  
The American Association of Cardiovascular and Pulmonary  
Rehabilitation and the ATS are working to address this imbalance 
between need and utilization of PR. Major initiatives include 
education of individuals with pulmonary diseases as well as health 
care providers caring for them of the value of PR. The business 
model for PR does not adequately support free-standing PR cen -
ters, and thus too few PR centers exist to provide care to all who 
could benefit. As referral rates improve, novel methods to deliver 
PR are being developed utilizing telemedicine and remote patient 
monitoring for home- and community-based PR programs. 220 
T elehealth PR has been shown to be effective even for patients 
with severe COPD, 41 and it can result in significant improve -
ments in dyspnea, walk distance, and emotional status. 282  
The development of remote patient monitoring and wearable 
technology for patients with COPD is further opening channels 
to provide PR to those with limited access to brick-and-mortar 
PR programs.66 Insurance coverage for telehealth PR remains a 
challenge because it is not currently covered under Medicare  
or Medicaid, although some insurance companies (including 
Medicare and Medicaid) are reimbursing for remote patient 
monitoring.247 High rates of readmission are seen within 30 days 
of discharge following admission for a pulmonary exacerbation.

Summary of Pulmonary Rehabilitation
PR requires a dedicated and specialist team approach to the  
management of disability secondary to chronic lung disease.  
The rehabilitation program is ideally led by a PM&R physician 
with knowledge of the anatomy and pathophysiology of the car-
diovascular and respiratory systems as well as exercise physiology. 
The PM&R evaluation and treatment plan should reflect the di-
verse pulmonary pathology impacting function and the compre -
hensive team available for management. PR reflects a continuum 
of care from point of entry to the health system due to pulmonary 
disease presentation or exacerbation, utilizing all available reha -
bilitation services to return patients to their highest possible level 
of function in their community. While, ideally, PR is delivered in 
a dedicated PR setting, commonly it is practiced as a component 
of general rehabilitation where patients have pulmonary dysfunc-
tion as a complication of their neurologic or musculoskeletal 
disabilities or as a medical comorbidity. General rehabilitative 
therapeutic approaches apply in either setting. Health policy, 
legislation, and regulations, including current health care delivery

Organ Transplantation
Introduction
The idea of organ transplantation dates back to ancient mythol -
ogy and is described in Roman, Greek, Indian, Chinese, and 
Egyptian tales. The first written mention of an organ transplant is 
noted in the Ebers Papyrus, written circa 1550 BC, which men -
tions skin grafting for the treatment of burns.
195 Seventy years 
ago, the first successful organ (kidney) transplant was performed 
by Joseph Murray at the Peter Bent Brigham Hospital in Boston, 
Massachusetts. By the late 1960s, liver, heart, and pancreas trans-
plants from deceased donors were successfully performed, and 
lung and intestinal organ transplant procedures began in the 
1980s. The years since the first successful organ transplant have 
seen remarkable advances in surgical techniques, pre- and postop-
erative care, and development of potent immunosuppressant 
medications. In 1963, one of the most important landmarks in 
the history of organ transplantation was T om Starzl’s finding that 
adding prednisone to azathioprine for immunosuppression  
improved 1-year renal graft survival to greater than 70%.
234 This 
finding caused a sensation and completely changed the outlook 
for renal transplantation. The next landmark revolutionizing 
transplantation was the finding in 1976 that cyclosporine  
substantially improved kidney transplant outcomes.
35 Cyclospo-
rine soon became the standard baseline immunosuppressant. It 
remained this way until 1989 when it was found that tacrolimus 
could prevent rejection in organ allografts resistant to treatment 
by cyclosporine. Tacrolimus has now largely replaced cyclosporine 
as the baseline immunosuppressant agent.
234 These discoveries 
have resulted in markedly reduced morbidity and mortality in 
posttransplantation patients. Thousands of patients in the United 
States and around the world have benefited immensely from these 
advances in medical care by not only having their lives extended 
but also the quality of their lives.
Due to the multiple medical and functional complexities  
encountered when working with an organ transplant recipient,  
a team approach in managing these patients is critical. The  
transplant rehabilitation team consists of a physiatrist, physical 
therapist, occupational therapist, registered nurse, social worker, 
case manager, clinical pharmacologist, nutritionist, psychologist, 
and vocational therapist. The physiatrist’s role as team leader is to 
coordinate the interdisciplinary team. Constant communication 
with the primary transplant team and immediate discussion of 
any changes in the patient’s medical status is essential for optimal 
patient outcomes. The physiatrist and rehabilitation team should 
be knowledgeable of potential short- and long-term medical com-
plications posttransplant. The frequent and ongoing interaction 
with transplant recipients situates the rehabilitation team to be 
the early warning system for any deterioration in this patient 
population.
T ransplant recipients require immunosuppressant medications 
for life—typically, a combination of a calcineurin inhibitor (tacro-
limus [Prograf] or cyclosporine [Neoral]), an antimetabolite 
(mycophenolate mofetil [Cellcept]), and a steroid (prednisone). 
The physiatrist must be aware of the potential side effects that can 
occur with these medications and the multiple drug interactions 
that can occur with this triple therapy. In addition, organ trans -
plant recipients are considered to be at high risk for developing 
infections. This section will focus on the four most common 
transplantable organs seen in rehabilitation medicine: kidney 
(most commonly transplanted organ), heart, lung, and liver. For 
each of these transplantable organs, the pre- and postoperative 
rehabilitation management, postoperative medical complications, 
immunosuppressive and prophylactic medications utilized,  
and outcomes will be discussed. In addition, the key role that re-
habilitation plays in restoring the organ transplant recipient’s 
functional potential and maximizing patient quality of life will be 
highlighted.
Renal Transplantation
Introduction
Renal transplantation is the treatment of choice for patients with 
end-stage renal disease (ESRD). ESRD is total and permanent 
kidney failure requiring dialysis or transplantation for survival. 
Indications for renal transplantation and contraindications for 
renal transplantation are outlined in Boxes 29.4 and 29.5.
Epidemiology
The kidney is the most commonly transplanted major organ. In 
2021, 25,549 kidney transplants were performed, of which 
19,569 were from deceased donors and 5979 were from living 
donors. The number of patients with a functioning kidney trans-
plant increased to 245,506 by the end of 2021, an increase of 
39.1% over the decade. However, only approximately 30% of 
ESRD patients had undergone a kidney transplant, a percentage 
that remained virtually unchanged over the decade. Overall, 
White and Asian patients consistently had the highest rates of 
kidney transplantation. Hispanic, Black, Native American, and 
Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander patients had lower rates. Kidney 
Indications for Renal Transplant
Any condition that leads to end-stage renal (kidney) disease:
• Diabetes
• Hypertension
• Prerenal (chronic or acute ischemia)

Etiology of End-Stage Renal Disease
Diabetes and hypertension are the two leading causes of ESRD in 
the United States and worldwide. Other diseases leading to  
serious kidney dysfunction and for which renal transplantation 
may be required are grouped into prerenal (chronic or acute  
ischemia), intrinsic renal (glomerulonephritis, focal-segmental 
glomerulosclerosis), and postrenal (reflux nephropathy, obstruc -
tion) categories.4
Preoperative Rehabilitation Management
When prescribing a rehabilitation program for potential kidney 
transplant patients (preoperative or postoperative), one must  
consider their markedly impaired cardiorespiratory and musculo-
skeletal status. Physical activity, cardiorespiratory fitness, muscle 
mass, and bone mineral density decrease even in the early stages 
of chronic kidney disease (CKD) and continue to decrease with 
disease progression. The combined effects of uremia and physical 
inactivity drive the loss of muscle mass.281 The markedly impaired 
exercise tolerance that these patients experience not only inter -
feres with their ability to perform physical tasks such as climbing 
stairs but also interferes with their ability to perform leisure-time 
exercise. Their reduced physical fitness is characterized by reduced 
flexibility, coordination disturbances, and decreased muscular 
strength and endurance. 87 Decreased pretransplant physical  
function as measured by the Short Physical Performance Battery 
(which measures balance, chair stands, gait speed, and grip 
strength) has been shown to be associated with a longer length of 
hospital stay and rehospitalizations following kidney trans -
plant.157,177 It has also been shown to be associated with a 2.3-fold 
increased risk of posttransplant mortality.178
In addition, kidney transplant recipients (KTRs) have specific 
features that distinguish them from other SOT recipients. These 
need to be kept in mind when prescribing a rehab program  
for these patients. First, about half of all patients with ESRD 
worldwide are over 65 years of age. This results in a higher propor-
tion of older KTRs compared with other SOT recipients.  
Next, advancements in dialysis techniques allow for a variable, 
sometimes very long, waiting times on dialysis. The majority of 
deceased-donor KTRs require a waiting time of 2 to 4 years, com-
pared with 0 to 5 months for liver, heart, and lung transplant 
recipients. This chronic disease burden has a great impact on the 
progression of comorbid conditions and quality of life.208 It is felt 
that kidney transplant candidates are uniquely suited for rehabili-
tation because they experience this profound loss of functional 
capacity while waiting years for transplantation while on dialysis. 
McAdams-Demarco et al. found that 2 months of prehabilitation, 
consisting of weekly outpatient rehabilitation, improved physical 
activity by 64% and decreased posttransplant length of stay when 
compared with age-, sex-, and race-matched controls. In addition, 
they stressed the importance of optimizing metabolic and  
nutritional status along with promoting physical activity. 170 For 
patients with all stages of CKD, it has been shown that regular 
physical activity benefits cardiometabolic, neuromuscular, and 
cognitive function.281
Postoperative Medical Issues
Despite the benefits of kidney transplantation for end-stage renal 
failure, cardiopulmonary and musculoskeletal dysfunctions are  
a continued concern after surgery. 75 In addition, as with any  
intraabdominal procedure, patients undergoing kidney transplant 
have impaired postoperative pulmonary function due to general 
anesthesia and diaphragmatic inhibition.204
Postoperative muscle weakness and reduced exercise tolerance 
are also seen after surgery.262
It is unclear whether this is a result of disease-related changes 
in skeletal muscle physiology or due to a reduction in physical 
activity posttransplantation. In addition, the patient’s reduced 
bone mineral density continues to be a major problem after trans-
plantation. This is a result of suboptimal kidney function and the 
superimposed effects of steroids on bone. A reduction in bone 
mineral density has been found in 60% of patients in the first  
18 months after renal transplantation. 61 The estimated fracture 
rate after transplantation is 2% per year in nondiabetic patients, 
5% per year in diabetic patients, and 12% per year in pancreas-
kidney recipients.
Infections are a common complication following kidney trans-
plantation. Infectious complications are categorized as occurring 
in one of three time periods posttransplant: early posttransplant 
infections, infections during peak immunosuppression, and  
late-onset infections. Most infections in the first month after 
transplant are health care–associated infections, whereas late  
infections, beyond 6 to 12 months, are community-acquired  
infections. Early posttransplant infections occur in the first  
30 days posttransplant. The majority of such infections (approxi-
mately 98%) are common postsurgical infections, including sur -
gical site infections, pneumonias, urinary tract infections (UTIs), 
bacteremia, and Clostridioides difficile colitis. The second period of 
posttransplant infections occurs during peak immunosuppression 
(between 30 days and 6 months posttransplant) or within  
3 months of treatment of rejection. They are typically opportunis-
tic infections or pathogens that reactivate from latent infection in 
the recipient, such as BK polyomavirus, CMV , herpes simplex 
virus, varicella zoster virus, hepatitis B virus, hepatitis C virus, 
tuberculosis, listeria, strongyloidiasis, and Chagas disease. Late-
onset infections typically present greater than 6 to 12 months 
posttransplant or greater than 3 months after treatment for a  
rejection episode. Most late-onset infections are community  
acquired, such as community-acquired pneumonia, respiratory 
viral infections, and UTIs.6
Postoperative Rehabilitation Management
The goal of rehabilitation following renal transplantation is to 
improve patients’ physical and psychological health for them to 
achieve a level of fitness that will allow them to maintain an active 
lifestyle. A meta-analysis and systematic review of the effects of 
exercise training in kidney transplantation recipients revealed  
that exercise training resulted in significantly improved exercise 
tolerability as measured by VO 2 peak and quality of life. 187 In a 
randomized double-blind study, post–renal transplantation pa -
tients undergoing an early physiotherapy program from the first 
postoperative day until hospital discharge had significantly higher 
respiratory muscle strength at hospital discharge when compared 
with a control group. 188 A study by Zhu et al. found that early 
mobilization after renal transplantation had multiple benefits 
compared to a control group. The early mobilization group
