increased both vagal and sympathetic cardiac modulation while 
shifting the autonomic balance toward greater vagal control. The 
majority of RCTs indicate that exercise training improves health-
related quality of life in KTRs, irrespective of the type of training 
or the time after transplantation.73
Outcomes
The prognosis after kidney transplantation is excellent. For those 
transplanted in 2020, the 1-year patient survival among living 
donor recipients was 98.1%, and 92.6% for deceased donor  
recipients. The 5-year patient survival for living donor recipients 
was 87.2%, and 76.3% for deceased donor recipients. 258 Despite 
this, kidney transplantation recipients experience a variety of chal-
lenges in adjusting to daily life activities as well as dealing with the 
social consequences and emotions related to their transplantation 
and the donor. A cross-sectional study to evaluate the different 
components of physical performance and quality of life in a  
cohort of kidney-transplanted patients revealed the presence of 
significant limitations in physical, social, and emotional function-
ing within the first 6 months after transplantation. This was felt 
to be due to factors related to the hospitalization and to prophy -
lactic restrictions (use of a face mask, frequent hand washing, 
avoidance of contact with animals, etc.). The authors stress that a 
systematic assessment for every kidney transplant patient should 
include an evaluation of physical performance and quality  
of life.81
Older KTRs (ages 65 to 80 years) often feel constrained by 
slow recuperation and overwhelmed by unexpected comorbidi -
ties, medication-related side effects, and health decline. There is a 
need to prepare and support older recipients for self-management  
responsibilities, clarify their expectations of posttransplant risks 
and outcomes, and aid throughout the prolonged recovery  
after kidney transplantation. 197 It has been shown that kidney 
transplant patients who underwent an empowerment education 
program showed a decrease in uncertainty and an improvement in 
self-care ability and compliance.130 In addition, patients also have 
to deal with the challenge of complex drug regimens. A study 
aimed to evaluate the behavioral and physiologic outcomes of 
pharmaceutic care in this population revealed that consistent 
posttransplant pharmaceutic care is effective to substantially  
improve knowledge following transplantation. 276 Guidance was 
consistently provided to patients so they could gain knowledge  
of their medications and side effects as well as understand the 
importance of rejection prevention. Interventions included face-
to-face interviews, checkups for laboratory examinations, discov -
ery of drug-related problems, and pharmaceutic consultation. The 
authors concluded that pharmaceutic care should be started as 
early as possible during the pretransplant period and continue in 
a long-term follow-up.
Patients with kidney failure treated with dialysis or kidney 
transplantation experience difficulties maintaining employment 
due to the condition itself and the treatment. A systematic review 
and meta-analysis found that patients with kidney failure had a 
low employment rate during dialysis and pre- and posttransplant. 
The employment rate for dialysis patients was 26.3%, pretrans -
plant patients 36.9%, and posttransplant patients 38.2%. In the 
meta-analysis, the strongest predictor of posttransplant employ -
ment was pretransplant employment. Younger patients and those 
with higher educational levels were more likely to be employed 
posttransplant. The authors concluded that it is important to sup-
port kidney failure patients through a combination of clinical and 
social measures to ensure that they remain in work. 134
Future
Since the first successful kidney transplant in 1954, outcomes 
have steadily improved through advances in immunosuppressive 
medications, tissue typing, and organ preservation techniques. 
Kidney transplantation is associated with much better long-  
term outcomes than any other modality of renal replacement  
and should be the goal for the majority of patients with ESRD. 
Despite this, the growth of kidney transplantation has not kept  
up with the growth in the ESRD population. The organ shortage 
has led to patients waiting many years on dialysis with increased 
mortality. According to the US Renal Data System 2023 Annual 
Data Report, by the end of 2021 72,864 patients with ESRD 
were on the waitlist for a kidney transplant. One year after being 
on the kidney transplant waitlist, 12.3% of patients had received 
a living donor transplant, 15.2% had received a deceased donor 
transplant, 1.7% had died, and 3.6% had been removed from the 
waitlist. Thus 67.2% were still waitlisted and receiving dialysis  
1 year after being placed on the waitlist. Five years after being 
waitlisted for a kidney transplant, 46.3% of patients had received 
a transplant, whereas 18.5% had died. 258 The main challenge for 
the future will be to increase the number of kidney transplants 
and reduce the gap between supply and demand. Strategies in -
clude promotion of living donations, changes made to the kidney 
allocation system in an attempt to allocate available kidneys with 
increased equity and efficiency, and the use of organs from donors 
with hepatitis C or HIV .58 Other challenges include increasing the 
focus on improving quality of life and employment opportunities 
for patients following kidney transplantation.
Heart Transplantation
Epidemiology
Congestive heart failure (CHF) affects approximately 6.7 million 
Americans over the age of 20 years, and this number is expected 
to increase to 8.5 million by the year 2030.37 Cardiac transplanta-
tion offers the survival and quality of life benefit and remains the 
gold standard in treatment of select patients with end-stage heart 
failure where medical management fails. The biggest challenge in

Owing to advances in medical therapy for patients with CHF 
as well as the increased use of mechanical circulatory support 
implantable cardiac devices, the annual mortality rate while on 
the transplant waitlist has progressively declined from 15% to 
20% to 7% to 10% since 2006. One-year survival for candidates 
on the waitlist increased from 34.1% for 1987-1990 to 67.8% for 
2011-2017. The new allocation system implemented in 2018  
resulted in improved waitlist survival. In addition, long-term 
outcomes after cardiac transplantation have improved with ad-
vances in candidate selection criteria, surgical techniques, infec-

and rehabilitation.

57 years, but the proportion of recipients at extremes of age has 
increased.
Other important changes in the demographics of heart trans -
plant recipients over the past 2 decades include increased recipient 
BMI, increased proportion of females, and increasing proportion 
of transplant patients with diabetes, previous dialysis, hyperten -
sion, previous cardiac surgery, presence of mechanical circulatory 
support, and previous malignancy.
129 The median survival after 
heart transplantation is 12.4 years. If recipients survive past 1 year, 
they have a greater than 50% chance to survive at 14 years.
168
Adult heart transplant recipient survival is around 90% at  
1 year, 85% at 3 years, and 80% at 5 years. 108 The 30-day opera-
tive mortality for cardiac transplantation spans from 5% to 10% 
because of graft failure, multisystem organ failure, and infection. 
Graft failure, acute rejection, and infection are the leading causes 
of death during the first year. Cardiac allograft vasculopathy and 
malignancy are the most significant causes of death after 1 year.

the American College of Cardiology and the American Heart  
Association are shown in Box 29.6. The absolute and relative 
contraindications are listed in Box 29.7.

Rehabilitation Before Heart Transplantation
The inciting etiology that leads to CHF includes a broad differen-
tial, including ischemic heart disease, valve dysfunction, idiopathic 
cardiomyopathy, or viral myocarditis. Evaluations by both a cardi-
ologist and a physiatrist should be integrated to better understand 
the disease process and its probable impact on the patient’s quality 
of life pre- and posttransplantation. The prioritization of appropri-
ate candidates for transplantation is based on medical comorbidities 
as well as the expected gains in quality of life compared with  
the optimization of medical and surgical resources available. 
Geographic distance between donor and potential recipient is also 
a consideration. Patients awaiting transplantation may be reas -
sessed every 3 months for quantification of circulatory impair -
ments, exercise performance, comorbid diagnoses, and overall 
health. The most successful outcomes depend on the identifica -
tion of candidates who have the physical capacity to maximally 
benefit from a new heart and the rehabilitation process.

Immediately after the surgery, right- and left-sided filing pressures 
within the heart are elevated, which results in myocardial anoxia 
and PH that will eventually normalize over a few weeks. 127 Post-
operatively, left ventricular ejection fraction and myocardial con -
tractility are within normal limits or slightly depressed. With the 
loss of vagal tone, the denervated heart has a higher than normal 
resting heart rate, which is usually 15 to 25 beats per minute 
above average consistent with the intrinsic rate of the sinoatrial 
node of the transplanted heart. This resting tachycardia is associ -
ated with a reduced stroke volume to maintain a near-normal 
resting cardiac output. Vagal maneuvers such as carotid massage 
or Valsalva does not affect the heart rate. Owing to its denerva -
tion, a transplanted heart increases to a maximum heart rate as 
well as return to baseline more slowly than a normal heart by  
a process that is principally mediated by the rise and fall of circu -
lating catecholamines, although a minority of patients demon -
strate signs of partial cardiac reinnervation several months after 
surgery.233
Despite this, cardiac output in the transplanted heart increases 
with total body activity mainly by the Frank-Starling mechanism, 
which stimulates venous return increasing cardiac preload,  
resulting in greater stroke volume. 127 Thus the response of the 
transplanted heart to exercise is preload dependent at the onset of 
increased exertion, but it switches to a catecholamine-driven pro-
cess through prolonged exercise that is unable to make sharp 
fluctuations in heart rate response. When the cardiac transplant 
recipient begins exercise, the Bainbridge reflex mediates a small 
rise in heart rate as a response to increased pressure in the right 
ventricular veins that can continue for up to 5 minutes, which can 
also contribute to a slow return to preexercise heart rate. The peak 
heart rate and systolic blood pressure attained during maximal 
exercise is lower in cardiac transplant recipients, only reaching to 
80% of normal.45,127 Because of this chronotropic incompetence, 
it has historically been presumed that heart transplant recipients 
would not be able to fully adapt to high-intensity interval training 
and would be best served by enrollment in a steady-state intensity 
exercise regimen. 185 The patient must therefore be educated to 
begin any workout with a slow warmup period and a graded pro-
gression of activity intensity.
The transplanted heart compensates for output demand mainly 
by increasing stroke volume. At the onset of exercise, stroke  
volume in the transplanted heart can rapidly increase to 20%. 149
Successive increases in stroke volume and cardiac output during 
sustained submaximal exercise result from inotropic responses to 
circulating catecholamines.149 Table 29.6 summarizes the effects of 
cardiac transplant on various cardiovascular variables. Owing to 
this catecholamine-induced tachycardic response to increased exer-
tion, exercise prescriptions based on target heart rates are not sug-
gested. More useful determinants of exercise intensity include 
blood pressure reserve (the difference between systolic and diastolic 
pressure), the Borg scale of perceived exertion (a categoric scale 
from 1 to 10, with 10 representing the maximal exertion), and the 
dyspnea index (a scale from 0 to 4, with 4 being the maximum 
shortness of breath that prevents counting or speaking).145,217 Rest-

premorbid elevations in vascular resistance secondary to hypersen-

failure.127 Diastolic blood pressure can decrease early in submaxi -

infection, neurotoxicity, renal toxicity, hypertension, and various

to 20% of perioperative deaths of heart transplant patients,  
with the most important causes resulting from dysfunction of the 
donor myocardium from acute rejection, PH, and ischemic in -

and mortality in the setting of increased afterload resulting in 
ventricular failure from pulmonary vascular resistance. The heart 
often requires inotropic support during the period immediately 
after transplant. Owing to autonomic denervation, the transplant 
recipient may be subject to multiple types of arrhythmias, includ-
ing atrial fibrillation, flutter, supraventricular tachycardia, or 
bradyarrhythmias.253 Cyclosporine-induced renal vasoconstric -
tion superimposed on chronic renal hypoperfusion may result  
in hypertension through glomerular arteriolar vasoconstriction.  
Tacrolimus (Prograf, Advagraf, Protopic) is associated with a 
lower incidence of hypertension compared with cyclosporine, 
which may be used under the direction of the transplant team. 
Blood pressures should be closely monitored, intervening with 
antihypertensive agents as needed. In most circumstances, this 
can be achieved without interruption of the exercise therapy regi-
men. Moderate to severe hypertension can affect up to 90% of 
cardiac transplant patients. Diuretics and beta blockers should be 
used cautiously because of volume depletion/hypotension and 
blunting of the heart rate response to exercise. 253
Careful assessment of neurologic and mental status as well as 
motor function is an essential part of the physiatric consultation 
in the postoperative phase. Neurologic complications that can 
occur acute posttransplantation include metabolic encephalopa -
thy, central nervous system infection, seizures, and psychosis. 
Strokes can occur from particulate or air embolism and cerebral 
hypoperfusion during the surgery. One of the leading causes of 
death in post–cardiac transplant patients is infection, including 
mediastinitis, pneumonia, UTI, or intravenous catheter-induced 
sepsis. Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Staphylococcus 
have been shown to be the cause of the majority of bacterial infec-
tions in this patient population. However, CMV is the most sig -
nificant cause of morbidity and mortality related to infection. 
CMV is associated with acute rejection, acceleration of coronary 
artery disease, and lymphoproliferative disease. CMV prophylaxis 
and active treatment include agents such as ganciclovir (Cytovene, 
Cymevene). Aspergillus is an opportunistic pathogen with the 
highest mortality and can cause serious pneumonia in 5% to 10% 
of recipients during the first 3 months posttransplant. 253
The incidence of Pneumocystis carinii  pneumonia can range 
from approximately 1% to 10%. T oxoplasmosis gondii infection is 
a result of reactivation of latent disease in the seropositive donor 
heart and can cause significant infections of the central nervous 
system, which require treatment with pyrimethamine (Daraprim), 
sulfadiazine (Lantrisul, Neotrizine), or clindamycin (Cleocin).253

Therapeutic Exercise After Cardiac 
Transplantation
Aerobic cardiovascular conditioning programs and endurance 
training improve the ability of the heart transplant recipient to 
accomplish sustained involvement in higher levels of ADLs.

Regular exercise training can be effective in improving exercise 
capacity, particularly a regimen of at least 12 weeks in duration,

transplant surgery. Enrollment into a supervised moderate-inten-

has shown to improve VO

when compared with those recipients who did not enroll, but

age-controlled patients.
75,113,186
Both cardiac hemodynamic and peripheral physiologic factors 
most likely contribute to this reduction in exercise capacity  
following heart transplantation. The cardiac factors include  
chronotropic incompetence, possible impaired left ventricular func-
tion, or greater arteriovenous oxygen difference. Peripheral factors 
include decreased muscle mass, anabolic resistance as a result of 
reduced strength and oxidative capacity, or impaired vasodilatory 
capacity. Other issues to consider as possible etiology for  
decreased exercise capacity include advanced age of the patient, donor 
heart age, operative ischemic time, or premorbid deconditioning and 
comorbidities.
185 Only a few RCTs have investigated the effect  
of exercise in heart transplant recipients. Most of these studies 
compared some form of rehabilitation or exercise program at 
moderate intensity with a control group without a specific form 
of an exercise strategy.
Resistance training has been shown to increase skeletal muscle 
mass and strength and should not begin until 6 to 8 weeks  
after transplantation, permitting time for sternal healing and  
corticosteroid tapering.
145,233,245 Studies demonstrate that heart 
transplant patients may be able to tolerate high-intensity interval 
training with benefits of overall improved exercise capacity, coro-
nary endothelial function, and blood pressure management, but 
future large-scale studies are needed to confirm these effects and 
determine the mechanisms by which they occur. High intensity 
interval training exercise results in beneficial effects, such as  
more substantial increase in peak VO
2 and lower extremity  
strength.109,139,145,185,186,232 Early in the history of cardiac trans -
plantation, it was considered inadvisable to start an exercise  
protocol immediately after the surgery. More recent research sug-
gests, however, that early mobilization and low-level exercise 
should begin in the hospital after extubation barring no medical 
contraindications.
128,232,233

Acute inpatient rehabilitation setting is often the preferred setting 
for postoperative heart transplant recipients as soon as relative 
medical stability is achieved. Ideal candidates for acute inpatient

All patients following heart transplantation should be referred 
to an outpatient cardiac rehabilitation program before or soon 
after their discharge from the inpatient setting. 217 Although 
almost every cardiac transplant patient faces episodes of acute 
cellular rejection (ACR), it is only rarely necessary to limit the 
exercise workout during episodes of moderate rejection. The risk 
of ACR is greatest within the first 3 months following 
transplantation. Greater than 60% of transplant recipients will 
have one or more episodes of rejection in the first 6 months. Risk 
factors for ACR include younger age at transplantation, female 
sex, Black race, and greater HLA mismatch. 72
When patients show signs of new arrhythmias, hypotension, 
or fever, the exercise regimen can be adjusted to allow for proper 
medical management. Clinical and physiologic monitoring of the 
patient and regular review of personal life and family goals are 
essential to maximize the patient’s prognosis, life plans, and family 
functioning. Patient and family education are crucial in transplan-
tation rehabilitation (Box 29.8).114
Lung Transplantation
In 2022, there were 2743 lung transplants and 45 heart/lung 
transplants performed in the United States. There were 3161 new 
adult candidates added to the lung transplant waitlist in 2022. 259 
Survival of lung transplant patients is improving but remains  
inferior to that of recipients of other SOTs, owing to the great 
number of complications in the first postoperative year. For lung 
transplants performed in 2021, the 1-year posttransplant mortal -
ity was 12.2%; for those performed in 2017, the 5-year posttrans-
plant mortality was 40.4%. 259 A significant factor in deciding 
which lung transplant procedure to perform is the underlying 
lung disease (Box 29.9). Lung conditions associated with chronic 
pulmonary infections (e.g., CF , bronchiectasis) must be managed 
with BLT to avoid spillover of infection from the native lung  
left in situ to the transplanted lung. While other indications,  
including interstitial lung disease, COPD, and pulmonary arterial 
hypertension, can be treated with single lung transplant, long-
term survival is enhanced with BLT , and that is the procedure of 
choice if there is availability of lungs for transplantation. 249,268 
Older patients with secondary PH also appear to have better  
outcomes from bilateral transplant. Updates to the international

success with the transplant surgery and refer for pretransplant  
PR. Indeed, the astute lung transplant listing committee will en -
gage the PR team in the decision to list a patient or not. Patient 
factors integral to the PR team’s skill set include strength, endur-
ance, compliance, nutritional, emotional, and cognitive status.  
All these factors impact a patient’s suitability for and success with 
LTx. Despite PR programs pre- and posttransplantation being 
considered optimal management for lung transplant patients, 
there remains a paucity of randomized controlled studies looking 
at functional outcomes. Pretransplant PR is associated with im -
provement in patient’s medical status, strength, and function as 
well as improved outcomes following LTx.
3,271 It has been noted 
that a pretransplant PR program will have positive effects on ex -
ercise capacity, specifically in functional domains of physical 
functioning, vitality, social functioning, and mental health.
85
It is important to appreciate that patients listed for lung trans-
plant have advanced/end-stage lung disease and often significant 
comorbidities and do not present like standard PR patients. 
While the pretransplant PR program has the same basic exercise 
elements, including resistance strength training, postural and  
balance exercises, breathing retraining and chest therapies, and 
progressive aerobic endurance training, lung transplant patients 
typically have more advanced disease and disability and require 
more individualized attention. Patients should undergo the com -
prehensive pre-participation PR team evaluation as described 
earlier, including 6MWT and CPET if able to tolerate. Patients 
requiring oxygen for activity and exercise will not be eligible  
for metabolic testing due to the need for supplemental oxygen 
during ESTs. Due to weakness and fatigue, individual PR sessions 
may require more frequent rest breaks, and the length of the 
session can be extended to accommodate this. Focus on  
energy conservation training and pacing is essential. Nearly all 
patients pending lung transplant will be oxygen dependent, and 
attention is essential to ensure optimal oxygen delivery during the 
PR sessions as well as in the community. Although goal oxygen 
saturation is greater than 88% with activity and PR training, it is 
not unusual for saturations to dip into the 70% range; every effort 
should be made to limit such desaturations. Functional progress 
may be slower, and the rate of exercise progression built into the 
PR program should reflect this. A greater emphasis on nutrition 
evaluation and therapy is also required because many patients 
may be poorly nourished contributing to functional limitations. 
Anxiety, depression, and adjustment disorder are frequent in pa -
tients pending lung transplant, and there is often a greater need 
for psychological and supportive therapies. Insurance will cover 
outpatient PR for a limited period of time, and while it is not 
known exactly when a suitable organ will be available for trans-
plant, coordination between the transplant team, patient, and PR 
team should aim to engage patients in PR up to the time of the 
transplant. Emphasis on home and community-based PR is  
essential to support the gains achieved at the PR center.
Posttransplant Rehabilitation: Medical 
Complications
Posttransplant PR starts immediately when the patient is deemed 
medically stable postop. ICU early mobilization programs for surgi-
cal populations have been shown to minimize functional loss,  
reduce ICU and surgical floor length of stay, and patients return  
to the community at a higher functional level.

following lung transplant will require inpatient rehabilitation.

Understanding the natural history of chronic lung disease that 
patients often medically and functionally deteriorate before being 
eligible for lung transplant, and as well as the reality for many 
patients that they spend months or years on a lung transplant list, 
frequently necessitates pretransplant PR. PR professionals should 
be knowledgeable of the indications and basic criteria for trans -

transplant center. Most transplant programs prefer to see patients 
earlier in the course of their lung disease and follow patients  
over time to optimize the timing of the transplant. T ransplant

This is best initiated in the acute hospital setting and continued in 
a dedicated inpatient rehabilitation facility–level setting familiar  
for managing post–lung transplant patients. The most significant 
difference pre- to posttransplant is supplemental oxygen, which 
should be no longer needed postoperatively. Patient characteristics 
supporting the need for acute inpatient PR are similar to other in-
patient rehab programs, including a multidisciplinary rehabilitation 
team need, medical complexities precluding discharge to home, and 
the ability to tolerate and make progress with at least 3 hours of  
PR daily. Length of stay can vary from 1 to 3 weeks on average. 
Ongoing engagement of the lung transplant team is essential for 
optimal medical management, readmission reduction, and care 
coordination post PR. Goals of inpatient PR include medical stabil-
ity, functional safety with ADLs and ambulation, and patient and 
family/caregiver education. Successful transition to the community 
requires a dedicated transplant social worker/case manager.
Homecare services provide a bridge between inpatient and 
outpatient PR. The goal is to enroll patients in outpatient PR as 
soon as possible. Medical clearance from the lung transplant team 
is required. Patients on outpatient PR following lung transplant 
are immune suppressed, and infection prevention is paramount. 
Hand hygiene is essential, and respiratory protection with a mask 
is often required initially. As mentioned, typically oxygen therapy 
is not required posttransplant, and patients steadily progress with 
strength and endurance. Overall function can rapidly improve 
and, in parallel, the patient’s emotional state. Goals of post–lung 
transplant outpatient PR include improved strength and endur -
ance for all ADLs and community participation, improved  
nutrition and emotional status, education on self-care and post -
transplant medical requirements, and integration of a lifelong 
healthy lifestyle, including ongoing exercise in the community. 
Return to avocational activities is frequently achieved, but  
although close to 80% of lung transplant patients had worked at 
one point prior to their transplant, only 15% were working at the 
time of transplant, and just 28% worked within 5 years of LTx.175 
A recent review of employment following heart and LTx indicates 
improved employment rates associated with younger age, higher 
education levels, and a history of pretransplant employment. 121 
Engagement of the vocational therapist as part of the rehabilita -
tion team is essential to support return to vocational activities. As 
the PR team is engaged in the lung transplant recipient’s care over 
the course of many months, the rehabilitation professional plays 
an integral part in patient monitoring for the early and late 
medical complications that can arise after LTx.90 Improvements in 
surgical techniques, lung preservation, immunosuppression, and 
management of infections have significantly improved the short-
term mortality. Bronchiolitis obliterans syndrome (BOS) and 
non-CMV infection account for the major causes of death after 
the first year.
Pulmonary complications after LTx have been described in 
detail133,246 and are summarized here:
Primary graft dysfunction. This is a syndrome of acute lung injury 
that occurs within 72 hours of a lung transplant, characterized 
clinically by pulmonary edema and pathologically by diffuse 
alveolar damage. Mild cases can resolve within 48 hours, but 
severe cases can persist for several weeks.
Airway-related and immediate postoperative complications. These 
include airway stenosis at the site of the anastomosis, which is 
the most common airway complication resulting from LTx 
and is seen in approximately 10% of cases. It develops typically 
around 3 months after surgery. Anastomotic dehiscence, due 
to ischemia, usually occurs 2 to 4 weeks after the transplanta -
tion and is reported in 1% and 10% of cases. Patients present 
with increasing dyspnea, pneumothorax, pneumomediasti -
num, subcutaneous emphysema, and persistent air leak from 
the chest tubes.
Infections. Although early-onset CMV infection is now very rare 
(3.3% of cases) due to prophylactic use of valganciclovir,  
delayed-onset CMV pneumonitis is more common (13.7% to 
25%) with increased risk of mortality and of BOS in those 
who recover. Aspergillus fumigatus is the most common fungal  
infection after lung transplant causing tracheobronchitis and 
invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. Bactrim prophylaxis has re -
duced the incidence of Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly known 
as P . carinii) infection, but it must be continued lifelong in 
lung transplant recipients; if stopped, 90% develop infection 
as evidenced by fever, dyspnea, and hypoxemia.
Immunologic complications (rejection). ACR occurs when re -
cipient lymphocytes react with donor antigens and is seen in 
35% of recipients within the first year of lung transplant. It 
accounts for less than 4% of deaths within the first month of 
transplant, but it is the most significant risk factor for the de -
velopment of BOS. ACR presents as dyspnea, cough, fever, 
and hypoxemia with a reduction in PFTs. BOS is characterized 
by irreversible, progressive airflow obstruction caused by the 
presence of obliterative bronchiolitis. It is a manifestation of 
CLAD, usually occurs within the first 3 months of LTx, and is 
responsible for the majority of deaths in the first year after 
transplant. It is the greatest threat to long-term graft and  
patient survival.
Neoplasms. Posttransplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD) 
ranges from benign polyclonal proliferation of lymphoid tissue 
to non-Hodgkin lymphoma and occurs in 2% to 8% of pa -
tients receiving lung transplants. Malignancy occurs at a 
higher rate in the native lung of patients receiving single-lung 
transplants with an incidence between 7% and 9%.
Iatrogenic complications. T ransbronchial biopsy, performed to 
evaluate rejection, causes focal areas of bleeding producing  
localized nodules and may persist for 1 month after biopsy. 
T oxicity to everolimus, an mTOR inhibitor used to prevent 
graft rejection, includes cytopenias, dyslipidemia, pneumoni -
tis, and renal and liver toxicity. Recurrence of the primary 
disease after LTx is most commonly seen in sarcoidosis in ap -
proximately 35% of cases and is seen as early as 2 weeks and as 
late as 2 years after the transplantation.
Nonpulmonary complications after LTx have been described 
in detail123,160 and are summarized here:
Renal failure. Some renal dysfunction is seen in over 25% at  
1 year and close to 40% at 5 years after lung transplant. 
Chronic renal failure (creatinine .2.5 mg/dL) is seen in nearly 
7% at 1 year and 11% at 5 years after transplant and increases 
the risk of death by four to five times. The cause of renal failure 
is due to calcineurin inhibitor nephrotoxicity. Other factors that 
worsen renal function include hypertension, hyperlipidemia, 
and diabetes. Management of kidney disease includes the  
lowest dose of calcineurin inhibitor tolerated and aggressive 
management of associated comorbidities. Chronic renal failure 
may progress and require dialysis or renal transplantation.
Diabetes. This develops in over 24% by 1 year and over 33% by 
5 years after lung transplant. Risk factors include glucocorti -
coid and calcineurin inhibitor use, older age of recipients, 
obesity with BMI above 30, and frequent acute rejection

fasting glucose levels greater than 126 mg/dL and hemoglobin 
A1c levels above 6.5%.
Cardiovascular disease. Immunosuppressants contribute to the 
development of cardiovascular risk factors, including hyper -
tension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, and renal disease. Hyperten-
sion is seen in over 50% of patients at 1 year and in over 85% 
at 5 years following lung transplant. Hyperlipidemia is seen in 
over 20% of patients at 1 year and over 50% at 5 years. How-
ever, CVD accounts for less than 5.3% of deaths in patients 
who survive longer than 1 year due to stringent pretransplant 
screening for CVD. Of interest, the use of statins within the 
first year of transplant to lower cholesterol also decreases acute 
rejection episodes and prevents BOS with better PFTs and 
improved mortality, which is likely due to the antiinflamma -
tory properties of statins.
Osteoporosis. This is most often due to the use of corticosteroids 
and other immunosuppressive medications. Pretransplant risk 
factors include tobacco use, prior corticosteroid use, and  
limited mobility. Patients with CF have increased risk due to 
hypogonadism, malnutrition, and impaired absorption of  
vitamin D and calcium. Osteoporosis is seen in 32% to 54% 
of pre–lung transplant patients. Bone loss is accelerated in the 
first 3 to 6 months after lung transplant. Within the first year 
of lung transplant, bone mineral density drops 4% to 12%, 
and the fracture rate ranges from 6% to 18%. Prevention and 
treatment of osteoporosis include calcium and vitamin D 
supplementation, regular weight-bearing exercises, estrogen 
and HRT (recognizing an increased risk of thromboembolic 
disease with HRT), calcitriol, and antiresorptive therapy  
with bisphosphonates. The greatest effect of bisphosphonates 
to reduce bone loss is seen when they are started before trans -
plantation. All pretransplant candidates should be screened for 
osteoporosis by dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry scan; anyone 
with osteopenia or osteoporosis should begin bisphosphonates 
and continue for 6 to 12 months after transplantation.
Avascular necrosis of the femoral head. This is seen in up to 10% 
of lung transplant recipients, is related to steroid use, and pres-
ents as hip and/or knee pain. Magnetic resonance imaging 
scans secure the diagnosis, and joint-sparing procedures such 
as core decompression may prevent total hip replacement.
Hematologic complications. Immunosuppressants, antivirals, and 
antibiotics suppress the bone marrow leading to cytopenia, the 
most common hematologic complication in lung transplant. 
Renal dysfunction also worsens anemia; erythropoietin with 
iron supplementation improves hemoglobin levels in anemic 
patients following lung transplant.
Thromboembolic complications. Deep venous thrombosis and 
PE are reported in 8.6% to 29% of lung transplant recipients. 
Risk factors include advanced age, diabetes, pneumonia, im -
mobility, and indwelling vascular catheters. T reatment includes 
intravenous heparin followed by long-term warfarin anticoag -
ulation. There is increased risk for pulmonary infarction due to 
altered pulmonary blood supply from the bronchial artery 
posttransplant. Placement of an inferior vena cava filter and 
lifelong warfarin therapy are often recommended.
Gastrointestinal complications. Ileus and colonic perforation are 
the most common gastrointestinal problems immediately 
posttransplant. At least 60% of patients have long-term com -
plaints, including nausea, vomiting, gastroesophageal reflux 
disease (GERD), diarrhea, constipation, and abdominal pain. 
Nausea is normally due to medications, but gastroparesis is 
common following lung transplant, seen in up to 25%. GERD 
is also common and can contribute to aspiration and BOS. 
Severe gastrointestinal complications include appendicitis, 
pancreatitis, cholecystitis, and diverticulitis.
Neurologic complications. These include severe headaches,  
seizures, strokes, and confusion in up to 26% of patients.  
Calcineurin inhibitor toxicity includes confusion, tremors, 
paresthesias, seizure, blindness, and encephalopathy.
Malignancy. There is a three- to fourfold increase in the risk of 
malignancy following lung transplant compared with the  
general population. The prevalence of malignancy in lung 
transplant patients is 3.7% at 1 year, 12.4% at 5 years, and 
25% in 10-year survivors. Malignancy accounts for nearly 
10% of deaths after the first year following transplant. PTLD 
is the most common cancer in the first 2 years after transplan-
tation, and skin cancers are the most common malignancy  
after the second year. Recommendations are for adherence to 
general cancer screening guidelines and evaluation by a derma-
tologist annually.
PTLD. This disorder is due to abnormal lymphoid proliferation 
and ranges from benign polyclonal hyperplasia to malignant 
monoclonal lymphoma. PTLD is seen in up to 20% of post–
lung transplant patients and usually involves the thorax and 
the transplanted lung. It most often occurs within the first year 
after transplant and is related to reactivation of the Epstein-
Barr virus. T reatment includes lowering immunosuppression 
and immunotherapy.
As patients survive longer after LTx, it is expected that pulmo-
nary and nonpulmonary complications will be seen with increas -
ing frequency. These complications can significantly impact 
strength, endurance, function, and quality of life; the rehabilita -
tion team plays a central role in early recognition and rehabilita -
tive management.
Postoperative Exercise Considerations
As discussed previously, early mobilization in the surgical ICU 
improves outcomes of patients undergoing cardiothoracic  
surgery.64 Progressive activity should be initiated as soon as the 
patient is deemed medically stable by the surgical/ICU teams 
progressing from ROM exercises and breathing/chest therapies to 
transfers, standing, and gait training. Progressive mobilization 
continues throughout the acute care stay with a goal of safety and 
minimizing requirements for assistance with ADLs and hallway 
ambulation. Some patients achieve independence with these ac -
tivities, as well as stair climbing, and can be discharged home 
from the acute care hospital. For many lung transplant recipients, 
functional progress is slow, and inpatient rehab, preferably at an 
IRF , is indicated to achieve functional goals. Progression to gentle 
endurance training with use of a treadmill or cycle ergometer is 
possible. T o reduce infection risk, the lung transplant recipient 
may be privately roomed while on the rehabilitation unit, and 
strict adherence to infection prevention policies is essential.
Following LTx, studies have shown substantial improvements 
in pulmonary function and exercise capacity, but peak exercise 
and quality of life related to physical functioning remain im -
paired. In other words, lung transplant recipients have an increase 
in exercise capacity below what would be expected considering 
the improvement in lung function, indicating that impaired  
skeletal muscle strength, cardiovascular deconditioning, and  
other peripheral factors play an important role in the limitation

post–lung transplant period may be reduced by up to 40% to

although the transplanted lungs are denervated, the ventilatory

during incremental exercise to approximately 60% to 70% of age-
predicted maximum, which would typically meet the patient’s 
workload demands. However, in patients with dual heart and 
lung transplants, resultant impairment in ionotropic and chrono-

30% to 50% of lung transplant patients will develop comorbid

alterations in muscle composition, with a reduction in type I 
muscle fibers likely stemming from premorbid sedentary lifestyle 
compounded by multiple hospitalizations. In lung transplant  
recipients, studies demonstrate a diminished capacity for  
oxidative metabolism manifested by activity-related early-  
onset lactic acidosis, decreased calcium uptake and release, early 
drop in muscle pH, and reduced peak oxygen consumption (VO2 
peak) in the peripheral muscles. This phenomenon may play a 
central role in limiting posttransplant peak exercise capability. 
Reduced oxidative capacity may also occur secondary to the  
effects of immunosuppressants, which can impair mitochondrial 
functioning.143,169,270
Referral to outpatient PR is essential for all patients after LTx. 
Participation in supervised rehabilitation programs improves skel-
etal muscle dysfunction and daily physical activity, which in turn 
improves overall quality of life and aids in reducing the risk of 
comorbid conditions after transplantation. There is literature to 
support the implementation of structured exercise training  
programs to optimize functional exercise capacity, skeletal muscle 
strength, and lumbar bone mineral density in lung transplant 
patients.271 A small RCT of 40 patients compared lung transplant 
recipients assigned after hospital discharge to either a structured 
3-month exercise training program or a control intervention and 
showed that after 1 year, the group that participated in the struc -
tured exercise training program had significant improvements in 
6-minute walking distance, quadriceps force, self-reported physi -
cal functioning, and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressures. 143 
Further studies are needed to develop standardized guidelines  
for exercise prescription in the lung transplant population.  
Challenges in standardization of exercise training for adults  
following lung transplant surgery is discussed in a recent  
Cochrane review.101 Lifelong exercise and adherence to healthy 
lifestyles is essential for longevity, well-being, and quality of life 
for individuals following lung transplant. It is the role of the PR 
team to instill and support this goal.
Liver Transplantation
Introduction
Liver transplantation is a standard proven therapy for irrevers -
ible liver disease. It is recognized as an effective and necessary 
treatment of chronic and acute hepatic failure. Advances  
in surgical techniques, immunosuppressive drugs, and pre-  
and postoperative care have resulted in liver transplantation 
becoming a well-established procedure that has saved innumer -
able lives worldwide.
Epidemiology
Liver transplant activity continues to increase in the United 
States. According to the Organ Procurement and T ransplantation 
Network (OPTN)/Scientific Registry of T ransplant Recipients 
(SRTR) 2022 Annual Data Report, in 2022, the number of liver 
transplants reached an all-time high of 9527, representing a 52% 
increase over the previous decade (2012-2022).142 Of these trans-
plants, 8924 (93.7%) were from deceased donors and 603 (6.3%) 
were from living donors. Liver transplant recipients were 94.5% 
adult and 5.5% pediatric. The overall size of the liver transplant 
waitlist contracted, with more patients being removed than 
added, although 10,548 adult patients remained on the waitlist at 
the end of 2022. Simultaneous liver-kidney transplant was the most 
common multiorgan combination, with 800 liver-kidney trans -
plants performed in 2022. Among adults added to the liver waitlist 
in 2021, 39.9% received a deceased donor liver transplant within  
3 months, 45.7% within 6 months, and 54.5% within 1 year.
Etiology
Alcohol-related liver disease is currently the most common  
indication for liver transplantation.56
Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) is the second most com-
mon indication for liver transplantation. 70 NASH includes a 
spectrum of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, ranging from steato -
sis alone to NASH with accompanying cirrhosis. These liver  
diseases are linked to metabolic syndrome with increased BMI 
and obesity. Since currently there is no effective treatment for 
NASH or fibrosis, liver transplantation has been rising due to 
NASH. Also, patients diagnosed with NASH with and without 
cirrhosis have an increased risk of developing hepatocellular carci-
noma (HCC). Until 2015, decompensated cirrhosis due to 
chronic hepatitis C infection was the most common indication 
for liver transplantation. However, it is now the third most com -
mon indication for liver transplantation, having been replaced by 
alcohol-related liver disease and NASH. 70 Other less common 
indications for liver transplantation include the following:
• Hepatitis B infection previously resulted in increasing num-
bers of chronic liver disease, leading to liver transplantation. 
However, the advent of the hepatitis B immunoglobulins 
and the introduction of antivirals have resulted in decreased 
rates of liver transplantation due to hepatitis B. 132
• Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) can lead to liver cirrhosis and 
failure, even with long-term corticosteroids and immuno -
suppression therapy. Liver transplantation is indicated in 
acute liver failure secondary to AIH or cases of chronic 
decompensated cirrhosis due to AIH.115
• Primary biliary cirrhosis patients with decompensated cir -
rhosis or severe pruritis refractory to other medical inter -
ventions require liver transplantation.148
• Primary sclerosing cholangitis currently has no effective 
medical therapy. Liver transplantation is considered an ef -
fective treatment modality among patients who develop 
decompensation or those who develop perihilar cholangio -
carcinoma (within certain criteria) or recurrent bouts of 
bacterial cholangitis.55
• HCC is one of the leading causes of cancer-related death 
worldwide, being the fifth most common cancer and the

incidence of HCC has been rising in the United States over

Patient Selection
The liver allocation system implemented by the OPTN in  
February 2002 is based primarily on the severity of liver disease as  
assessed by end-stage liver disease (MELD) and pediatric end-
stage liver disease (PELD) survival models for all patients with 
chronic liver disease.
138 The MELD score, which is based on 
biochemical variables, has been shown in retrospective and pro -
spective studies to be highly predictive of 3-month mortality in 
patients with chronic liver disease. The MELD score is limited to 
40 points and is calculated on the basis of the following variables: 
serum creatinine, serum bilirubin, and international normalized 
ratio. The MELD score ranges from 6 (less ill) to 40 (gravely ill) 
and is used for transplant candidates aged 12 years and older.  
The PELD score is used for transplant candidates younger than  
12 years of age.
29
Rehabilitation Management
Patient classification
In assessing the patient who is to undergo a liver transplant, it is 
important to recognize that patients may present dramatically 
differently in the extent of their disability depending on whether 
they have acute fulminant liver failure or chronic liver failure. 
Those with acute fulminant liver failure (e.g., secondary to acet -
aminophen overdose) often are otherwise healthy patients and 
present with limited functional deficits. In contrast, those with 
chronic liver failure (e.g., secondary to chronic hepatitis) have 
lived with their disease for many years and often present with se -
vere deconditioning, sarcopenia, and fatigue. Muscle strength in 
these patients has been found to be 30% of age-predicted levels.
29 
Sarcopenia and physical deconditioning are the end results of 
impaired dietary intake, chronic inflammation, altered macronu -
trient and micronutrient metabolism, and low physical activity. 
They are all associated with increased mortality in end-stage liver 
disease.
77 Patients with chronic liver disease have also been found 
to have a VO2 max that is 40% less than predicted for sedentary 
healthy individuals of the same age and sex. 30 Patients with

cirrhosis develop a global physical dysfunction associated with a 
multiplicity of factors, including muscle wasting, undernutrition 
and malnutrition, and functional impairment defined as frailty. 
Studies in this vulnerable population have demonstrated that the 
presence of frailty is independently predictive of adverse outcomes 
both pre- and posttransplantation. It is felt that its incorporation 
into clinical practice could result in improved clinical decision 
making, particularly regarding the identification of candidates for 
physical and nutritional interventions.209
Preoperative rehabilitation management
Pre- and postoperative management of the liver transplant recipi-
ent is a team effort that requires close collaboration between  
the surgeon, physiatrist, hepatologist, nephrologist, and other 
members of the rehabilitation team. It is critical to institute reha-
bilitation therapy as soon as possible to maintain relatively healthy 
levels of physical functioning and to improve survival before and 
after transplantation. A program of graded isometric exercises 
aimed at restoring muscle mass and strength, as well as ROM 
exercises and gentle stretching to prevent contractures, should  
be used preoperatively. The introduction of a respiratory physio -
therapeutic program for liver transplantation candidates has  
also been found to significantly improve respiratory parameters 
preoperatively. The program consists of diaphragmatic breathing 
exercises, diaphragmatic isometric exercises, and upper limb and 
abdominal strengthening exercises. 152 Preoperative clinical trials 
addressing the usefulness of exercise in patients with cirrhosis have 
shown that it improves the metabolic syndrome, sarcopenia, car -
diopulmonary endurance, health-related quality of life, and the 
hepatic venous pressure gradient. 77 A systematic review found 
that prehabilitation in patients actively listed for orthotopic liver 
transplant (OLT) improves aerobic and functional capacity.  
Significant improvements were observed in VO 2 peak, 6-minute 
walking distance, hand grip strength, liver frailty index, and qual-
ity of life. No serious adverse events were reported, and adherence 
to the training programs was high. The authors concluded that 
prehabilitation is safe and feasible in the OLT candidate and that 
all patients awaiting OLT , especially the most physically frail, 
should be enrolled in predefined prehabilitation programs. 118 A 
retrospective study evaluating the efficacy of a structured in-  
and outpatient exercise training program for cirrhotic patients 
who were referred for liver transplant evaluation compared groups 
who underwent the exercise program pretransplant (258 patients) 
vs. those who did not (200 patients). The results revealed a trend 
toward lower 90-day readmission rate in the exercise training group 
(17.9% vs. 20%) and shorter length of stay (14 vs. 17 days).9
Postoperative medical complications
In managing the individual who has undergone liver transplant, 
one must be aware of the potential postoperative complications. 
The most common complications in the liver transplant recipient 
include131 acute graft rejection, vascular thrombosis, biliary leak 
or stricture, infection, and adverse effects of immunosuppressant 
drugs. Most life-threatening complications associated with liver 
transplantation occur within the perioperative period. These in -
clude primary graft malfunction, acute rejection episodes, severe 
infections, and technical complications such as hepatic artery 
thrombosis and biliary leaks. Acute hepatic allograft rejection 
typically occurs within the first 7 to 14 days, and the risk is high-
est (40%) during the first 3 to 6 months after transplantation. 156 
Rejection is most commonly manifested by fever, malaise,  
right upper quadrant pain or tenderness, graft enlargement, and 
diminished graft function. A rise in bilirubin and transaminase 
levels is observed. With early detection, most acute rejection  
episodes can be treated successfully by augmentation of existing 
immunosuppressive medications or high doses of steroids. Post -
transplant infections (bacterial and fungal infections) are more 
common in the first month due to severe immunosuppression. 
After 1 year posttransplant, nonhepatic-related causes of death 
include malignancy (22%), CVD (11%), infection (9%), and 
renal failure (6%), whereas liver allograft failure accounts for less 
than one-third of deaths. 2 Post–liver transplant patients have an 
increased risk of acquiring COVID-19; however, they do not have 
a heightened risk of developing severe COVID-19 infection nor 
do they have an increased mortality.141
Postoperative rehabilitation management
Rehabilitation therapies should be instituted immediately after 
the transplant patient has been stabilized. Therapy is aimed at 
restoring muscle mass and strength and reversing the musculo -
skeletal changes resulting from inactivity and bedrest. Therapy 
begins with graded isometric and ROM exercises. Aerobic condi-
tioning exercises are especially important because a lower mean 
VO2 max is a significant predictor of poorer short-term survival. 
The introduction of an intensive early rehabilitation program for 
liver transplant recipients has been found to be well tolerated and 
feasible in the ICU. In a prospective randomized study evaluating 
liver transplant recipients over a period of 1 year, patients in the 
usual treatment group receiving one physiotherapy session a day 
were compared with those in an early and intensive rehabilitation 
group who received two sessions a day. Patients in the early inten-
sive rehabilitation group sat on the edge of their beds sooner  
(2.6 vs. 9.7 days; P 5 0.048), and their intestinal transit resumed 
earlier than patients in the usual treatment group. 162 In another 
study to evaluate the impact of early mobilization, it was found 
that following pediatric liver transplantation, the early mobiliza -
tion patient group regained the ability to walk without a rolling 
walker sooner and had a shorter length of hospital stay. 255
A physical exercise program for liver transplant patients has 
also been shown to lead to an improvement in functional capacity. 
In a study by Garcia et al., patients in the exercise group showed 
a 19.4% increase in the distance walked as well as an increase in 
their resting energy expenditure compared with a control group. 
The authors concluded that the exercise program promoted  
significant improvements in functional capacity and felt these 
findings had positive implications for the control of metabolic 
diseases, which are common in patients after liver transplanta -
tion.89 Combining supervised resistance training with aerobic 
exercises has also been found to be beneficial. In a study by Moya-
Nájera et al. 6 months after liver transplantation, patients were 
randomized into two groups: intervention group (IG) and control 
group (CG). The IG completed a 6-month program of moderate- 
to high-intensity exercises, and the CG followed usual care rec -
ommendations. The IG, compared with the CG, had significant 
improvements in aerobic capacity, hip extension, elbow flexion, 
overall maximal strength, physical functioning, and vitality 
health-related quality of life.174
Fatigue
Fatigue and reduced physical activity are major problems follow -
ing liver transplantation. 260 Fatigue is present in a considerable 
portion of recipients after liver transplantation, and almost half of 
them have severe fatigue. 111 A 12-week rehabilitation program, 
consisting of supervised physical exercise training and counseling

Furthermore, after the program, aerobic capacity and knee flexion

Nutrition
The importance of good nutrition combined with a rehabilitation 
program is critical to the successful outcome for a liver transplant 
recipient. Protein-energy malnourishment is commonly encoun -
tered in patients with end-stage liver disease who undergo liver 
transplantation. Malnutrition may further increase morbidity, 
mortality, and costs in the posttransplantation setting. Severely 
malnourished patients have a higher incidence of sepsis and death 
due to sepsis.277 It is essential to provide sufficient nutritional support 
during all phases of liver transplantation. Oral nutrition is preferred, 
but enteral nutrition may be required to provide the needed energy 
intake. Perioperative nutritional interventions in liver transplant  
recipients include synbiotics, micronutrients, branched-chain amino 
acid supplementation, immune-nutrition formulas, fluid and electro-
lyte balance, and the offering of nocturnal meals.103
Outcomes
According to the OPTN/SRTR 2022 Annual Data Report, patient 
survival after deceased donor liver transplant continues to improve, 
with 5.1% mortality observed at 6 months, 6.8% at 1 year, 12.7% 
at 3 years, 19.8% at 5 years, and 35.7% at 10 years. Five-year  
patient survival rates after living donor liver transplant exceeded 
those of deceased donor liver transplant. 142 A meta-analysis to 
evaluate alcohol relapse rate and its predictors after liver transplan-
tation found posttransplant alcohol relapse occurred in about  
one-fifth of patients who underwent alcohol-related liver transplan-
tation. Psychiatric comorbidities, pretransplant abstinence of less 
than 6 months, unmarried status, and smoking were associated with 
alcohol relapse after liver transplantation. Psychiatric comorbidities 
represented the strongest predictor of alcohol relapse. The authors 
concluded that close monitoring of psychiatric comorbidities and 
pretransplant alcohol abstinence for at least 6 months may decrease 
alcohol relapse after liver transplantation.57
In light of the improved survival rates, there has been an  
increased emphasis on returning to normal activity in the work -
place and on restoring social quality of life following liver trans -
plantation. The assessment of quality of life represents an ancillary 
tool to evaluate the efficacy of SOT in addition to graft and  
patient survival rates and complications. A systematic review of 
publications between 2001 and 2016 revealed that employment 
rates ranged from 26% to 80% pretransplant and 18% to 44% 
posttransplant. Improved survival following liver transplantation 
was not reflected in a return to employment, and retirement was 
common. The authors felt that interventions to minimize physical 
deconditioning and postoperative depression could improve the 
rate of return to employment. 265 Kang et al. found that up to 
50% of liver recipients remain unemployed mostly due to fatigue 
and muscle weakness. Therefore they stress the importance of re -
habilitation therapy aimed at reducing fatigue and increasing 
muscle mass after liver transplantation. 125 Emerging evidence 
suggests that continued patient information, structured psychoso-
cial support systems, and caregiver involvement may further help 
improve long-term health-related quality of life.2
Future
Due to the enormous progress made in technical skills as well  
as improvements in immunosuppressive medications and the 
management of postoperative complications, patients are living 
longer following liver transplantation. This prolonged longevity 
has brought about new concerns, such as the long-term effects of 
immunosuppression, as they relate to effects on the cardiovascular 
system, infections, and propensity for malignancy. In addition, 
despite improved longevity, improvements in quality of life and 
return to employment have not kept pace. Moreover, the demand 
for transplants continues to exceed the available supply. The con-
sequence of this disparity in organ need vs. availability is that 
many patients die on the waitlist every year. In the United States, 
one of four patients who are listed for liver transplant either die 
on the waitlist (12%) or become too sick to undergo the trans -
plant (13%).248 Expanding living donor transplantation remains 
a goal in countries where deceased donor liver transplant pre -
dominates, such as North America and Europe. However, trans -
plant from living donors is more complex and requires careful 
dissection. A living donor graft is partial, unlike the whole graft 
provided from a deceased donor.241 Another means of increasing 
the available deceased donors is by reducing organ discard rates. 
Advances such as machine perfusion systems, which preserve the 
graft by improving its oxygenation, offer great promise. Addition-
ally, using organs from viremic donors is now possible with the 
use of highly effective antiviral therapies in the recipient. Finally, 
xenotransplantation (transplanting livers from one species to  
another) has progressed recently, but many barriers remain.248
Rehabilitation Management  
of Diabetes Mellitus
Epidemiology
Globally, diabetes caused 1.5 million deaths in 2012 and an  
additional 2.2 million deaths from related conditions, such as 
cardiovascular and kidney diseases. Of these deaths, 43% occur 
before the age of 70 years. It is the eighth leading cause of death 
among both sexes worldwide.275
According to the National Diabetes Statistics Report, an  
estimated 38.4 million people had diabetes as of 2021. This com-
prises 11.6% of the US population. The prevalence of diabetes in 
adults over 65 years of age is 29.2%. For US adults age 18 years 
or older, for both sexes, diagnosis of diabetes was more prevalent 
in the American Indian and Alaska Native groups (13.6%), 
closely followed by non-Hispanic Blacks (12.1%), Hispanics 
(11.7%), non-Hispanic Asians (9.1%), and non-Hispanic Whites 
(6.9%). Factors associated with increased prevalence of diabetes 
included level of education, family income, and geographic prox-
imity to metropolitan areas. The incidence of diabetes in adults 
age 18 years or older was similar in 2021 (5.8 per 1000 adults) to 
2000 (6.2 per 1000 adults). However, for children and adoles -
cents, the incidence of both type 1 and especially type 2 diabetes 
increased significantly in 2002 to 2018. The main risk factors for 
diabetes-related complications in adults include smoking, over -
weight and obesity, physical inactivity, increased hemoglobin A1c, 
hypertension, and hyperlipidemia.
Comorbidities and Complications
In 2020, there were 16.8 million emergency department visits for 
adults with diabetes, and these included 267,000 for hyperglyce -
mic crisis, 240,000 for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), and 202,000 
for hypoglycemia.

In 2020, there were 7.86 million hospital discharges with  
diabetes as one of the listed diagnoses in adults, with 1.68 million 
for major CVDs, including ischemic heart disease and stroke,  
and 160,000 for lower extremity amputation. The data for 2017-
2020 revealed CKD in close to 40% of adults with diagnosed 
diabetes. Additionally, diabetes is the leading cause of vision dis -
ability with 10.1% reported severe vision difficulty or blindness in 
individuals with diabetes in 2021. Diabetes was the eighth leading 
cause of death in the United States in 2021. The total estimated 
costs of diabetes in the United States in 2022 was $413 billion. 
These costs include both direct medical costs and indirect costs 
related to loss of productivity, unemployment, and premature 
mortality. Given the prevalence of diabetes mellitus in the United 
States and globally, coupled with the significant impairments with 
which the condition is associated, it is not surprising that there is 
an increasing demand for the physiatrist to become well versed in 
the diagnosis, management, and rehabilitation of patients with 
diabetes mellitus.
Pathophysiology and Diagnosis
Diabetes can be grouped into four categories:
• Type 1 diabetes is caused by destruction of beta cells by an 
autoimmune process. This usually leads to complete deficiency 
of insulin.
• Type 2 diabetes is characterized by a dysfunction of insulin 
secretion by beta cells in the setting of insulin resistance and 
metabolic syndrome
• Specific types of diabetes are due to other causes:
• Monogenic diabetes syndromes. Examples of these include 
neonatal diabetes and maturity-onset diabetes of the young
• Diseases of exocrine pancreas, such as CF and pancreatitis
• Drug or chemical-induced diabetes, such as diabetes associ-
ated with glucocorticoid use, treatment of HIV/AIDS, or 
organ transplantation
• Gestational diabetes mellitus
At the time of the diagnosis, it is not always easy to discern 
type 1 and type 2 diabetes based on age alone because both can 
occur in adults and children. Presence and extent of beta-cell de -
ficiency can be inferred by screening for autoantibodies to insulin, 
glutamic acid decarboxylase, islet antigen 2, and zinc transporter 
8. The presence of these markers can help determine the type of 
diabetes, assess the risk for DKA and other complications, and 
assist in treatment selection.
Prediabetes and Diabetes Type 2
For the general population, screening for prediabetes and diabetes 
type 2 should begin at 35 years of age. However, those with a BMI 
of 25 kg/m2 or more or 23 kg/m2 or more in individuals of Asian 
descent and with one or more risk factors should be tested for 
diabetes regardless of their age. Risk factors include having a  
first-degree relative with diabetes, high-risk race/ethnicity (e.g., 
Black, Latino, Native American, Asian, Pacific Islander), history of 
CVD, hypertension, those with low high-density lipoprotein 
(,35 mg/dL) and high triglyceride ( .250 mg/dL), polycystic 
ovary syndrome, sedentary lifestyle, gestational diabetes, or HIV . 
Prediabetes is a risk factor for diabetes. It is characterized by 
impaired fasting glucose (100 to 125 mg/dL) and/or impaired 
glucose tolerance with a 2-hour glucose level from 140 to 199 mg/dL, 
and hemoglobin A1c of 5.7% to 6.4%. Those individuals with 
prediabetes should be counseled on risks of developing diabetes 
and risk of CVD and be provided education on strategies to lower 
these risks.80 The American Diabetes Association (ADA) guidelines 
for diagnosis of diabetes mellitus include:
• Hemoglobin A1c level $6.5%
• Fasting plasma glucose (FPG) $126 mg/dL
• Oral glucose tolerance test blood glucose at 2 hours  
$200 mg/dL
• Random plasma glucose test blood glucose $200 mg/dL
• Classic symptoms of hyperglycemia (polyuria, polydipsia, 
unexpected weight loss) or hyperglycemic crisis with a ran -
dom plasma glucose of $200 mg/dL ($11.1 mmol/L)
• Prediabetes: hemoglobin A1c of 5.7% to 6.4% or FPG  
of 100 to 125 mg/dL or 2-hour plasma glucose of 140 to 
199 mg/dL following an oral glucose tolerance test 15
Prevention Guidelines
Lifestyle Modifications
Current recommendations for the prevention of type 2 diabetes in-
clude lifestyle modifications, which include maintenance of healthy 
weight, proper and healthy nutrition intake, and participation in 
aerobic and resistance exercise programs.8 Some suggest there are  
additional benefits to a more comprehensive lifestyle modification 
program that incorporates behavioral components, such as tobacco 
cessation, cooking lessons, use of food diaries, and attendance at an-
nual diabetic education classes.218 Such programs should include an 
interdisciplinary team with physicians, case managers, nurses, exercise 
advisors and trainers, behavioral therapists and counselors, psycholo-
gists, dietitians, and physiotherapists. 218 Participation in such a  
comprehensive program has been shown to effectively reduce body 
weight and BMI in high-risk individuals and prevent type 2 diabetes, 
although advantages are less clear for people already diagnosed with 
the condition.218 In fact, participation in combined programs of di-
etary modification, aerobic exercise, and resistance training by at-risk 
individuals has been shown to reduce the incidence of type 2 diabetes 
by up to 58%.8
Treatment Guidelines
Exercise and Diabetes Mellitus
Given current recommendations for lifestyle modifications as the 
first line of diabetes prevention and treatment, the physiatrist is in 
a prime position to take leadership of management of patients 
with diabetes mellitus. Rehabilitation physicians are uniquely 
trained in musculoskeletal anatomy and function, exercise physi -
ology, and the use of physical activity as a treatment for disease. 
Further, the physiatrist is versed in the team approach to patient 
care, working closely with physiotherapists, exercise trainers, case 
managers, and nurses, as well as the individualization of patient 
care, taking into consideration the patient’s unique medical needs, 
impairments, goals, and social background.
Exercise is a major part of lifestyle changes for prevention  
and treatment of diabetes mellitus. The ADA recommends both 
aerobic and resistance training for adults with diabetes. This 
should include at least 150 minutes of moderate to vigorous aero-
bic exercise and two to three sessions of resistance training per 
week. The benefits of regular exercise include diabetes risk factor 
modifications, such as weight reduction, blood pressure reduc -
tion, improved insulin sensitivity and blood glucose levels, and 
improved lipoprotein numbers. Following exercise recommenda -
tions has been shown to decrease cardiovascular mortality and 
all-cause mortality.279

SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
Unfortunately, physical inactivity is prevalent in general in the 
United States. According to the CDC National Diabetes Statistics 
Report website, less than one-fourth of individuals with diabetes 
are exercising at or above the recommended 150 minutes per 
week.52
Assessment of Physical Activity
The American Heart Association recommends that physical  
activity (bodily movement produced by skeletal muscles that re -
sult in energy expenditure) and other cardiovascular risk factors 
be regularly assessed by clinicians. It is important to consider an 
individual’s overall physical activity profile, including all struc -
tured (e.g., exercise, planned activities) and incidental (e.g., 
ADLs, vocational, transport, unplanned activities) physical activ -
ity. Further, the dimensions (mode or type, frequency, duration, 
intensity) and domains (occupational, domestic, transportation, 
leisure) should be documented with each assessment. Both sub -
jective and objective methods of evaluation exist for clinical use. 
Physical activity questionnaires (PAQs) such as the Global PAQ, 
Short Recall PAQ, and quantitative history PAQs may be  
completed via personal or telephone interviews or self-reported. 
Additionally, physical activity diaries or logs such as the Bouchard 
Physical Activity Record are frequently used. Objective methods 
of quantifying physical activity, such as indirect calorimetry,  
the doubly labeled water method, direct observation, heart  
rate monitoring, or use of motion sensors (accelerometers and 
pedometers), may also be used by the clinician depending on 
availability.239
Physiologic Effects of Exercise in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
Individuals with diabetes mellitus typically present with limitations 
in physical fitness and activity, which contributes to the high car-
diovascular mortality seen in this population. Type 2 diabetes is 
associated with impaired glucose and fat metabolism as a result of 
impaired insulin sensitivity and lipid oxidation, as well as impaired 
myocardial and skeletal muscle perfusion likely resulting from vas-
cular endothelial dysfunction.210 Risk for type 2 diabetes increases 
with physical inactivity and occurs more frequency in individuals 
with obesity, hypertension, and dyslipidemia. The objectives of 
physical activity in the treatment of type 2 diabetes include  
improvement of diabetes-associated abnormalities in glucose,  
lipid, and blood pressure control and facilitation of weight loss and 
maintenance.60,124 During exercise, glucose uptake into actively 
contracting skeletal muscles is increased, which is balanced by  
hepatic gluconeogenesis and release of fatty acids. Additionally, 
muscle contraction appears to circumvent diabetes-associated insu-
lin resistance by inducing GLUT-4 translocation via a separate 
mechanism, which facilitates uptake of circulating glucose into  
the muscle. Insulin sensitivity following exercise can last up to  
72 hours. Regular activity also facilities beta-cell function, insulin 
sensitivity, vascular function, and even gut microbiota. 60,124,161,238 
The degree of glycemic reduction is related to the duration and in-
tensity of the exercise, preexercise glucose levels, and the individual’s 
state of physical training.60
Over the long term, both aerobic and resistance training  
result in increased muscle lipid storage, oxidation, and use. Type 
2 diabetes mellitus has been associated with a decrease in lipid 
oxidation and preferential use of carbohydrate for energy. Chronic 
benefits of exercise also include reduction of total and low-density 
lipoprotein cholesterol, elevation of high-density lipoprotein  
levels, reduction in systolic blood pressure, and reduction of  
depressive symptoms in both sexes of all age groups. Further, 
physical activity has been shown to improve vascular endothelial 
function, which may reduce incidence of complicating atheroscle-
rotic cardiovascular or peripheral artery disease, prevent the onset 
of diabetic peripheral neuropathy, and improve autonomic func -
tion in patients with diabetic autonomic neuropathy. Overall, 
physical activity and physical fitness are associated with lower 
all-cause and cardiovascular mortality.60 The exact mechanisms by 
which physical activity improves cardiovascular function in pa -
tients with type 2 diabetes remains unclear, but recent evidence 
shows that exercise is associated with reductions in inflammatory 
markers C-reactive protein (CRP) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) levels 
in individuals with type 2 diabetes mellitus. CRP is a strong inde-
pendent predictor of CVD and the outcome of acute coronary 
syndrome. CRP has long been identified to be generated in the 
liver in response to elevation in inflammatory cytokines, such as 
IL-6, but recent studies have shown production of CRP in both 
adipose tissue and atherosclerotic plaque.106
Preexercise Assessment
Low- to moderate-intensity exercise is likely safe and does not 
require special clearance. For those with a history of CVD or 
other diabetes-related complications, medical clearance is needed. 
Stress testing can be considered by the clinician to assess cardio -
vascular risk and overall level of fitness. Those with CVD may 
benefit from a more supervised training program, such as formal 
cardiac rehabilitation. Several potential complications should be 
kept in mind before prescribing an exercise program and guide 
patient education accordingly. Electrocardiogram stress testing 
may be indicated for patients over 40 years of age with or without 
CVD risk factors other than diabetes mellitus; for patients over  
30 years of age and with type 1 or 2 diabetes mellitus greater than 
10 years’ duration, hypertension, cigarette smoking, dyslipidemia, 
proliferative and pre-proliferative retinopathy, and nephropathy; 
and for patients with diagnosed or suspected coronary artery dis -
ease, cerebrovascular disease, peripheral artery disease, autonomic 
neuropathy, or advanced nephropathy with renal failure.59,60
Those with hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia risk may require 
medication dose adjustments, frequent blood glucose checks, 
counseling on carbohydrate intake prior to exercise, and hydra -
tion. Due to the risk of DKA in type 1 diabetes, testing for  
ketones is necessary with levels of blood glucose above 250 mg/dL. 
Exercise should be delayed if moderate to large amounts of  
ketones are present.
Types of Exercises for Patients with Type 2  
Diabetes Mellitus
The beneficial effects of physical exercise in the management  
of type 2 diabetes mellitus may result from aerobic or resistance 
activities. ADA and ACSM recommendations are outlined in 
Table 29.7 .60 Aerobic exercise improves insulin sensitivity in 
adults with type 2 diabetes, which may be related to improved 
mitochondrial function. This can occur with or without weight 
loss.
Resistance exercise training leads to 10% to 15% improvement 
in strength, bone density, insulin sensitivity, blood pressure, and 
lipids. As it turns out, a combination of aerobic and resistance 
training appears to be even more beneficial than each modality 
alone. According to the results of a meta-analysis comparing  
aerobic training, resistance training, and combined training  
across 14 trials, which included 915 participants, combined train-
ing leads to a larger reduction in hemoglobin A1c, glucose, and 
lipids.124,219

Flexibility exercise helps with mobilization of joints that can 
otherwise be limited by mechanisms of glycation, which result 
from aging and chronic hyperglycemia. While flexibility training 
does not impact glucose control, it can significantly improve  
participation and performance in exercise programs.
Balance exercises can be incorporated into other training mo -
dalities and can lower fall risk especially in those with peripheral 
neuropathy.
124
Although the guidelines provide a solid foundation upon 
which to build an exercise prescription for patients with type 2 
diabetes, it is important to individualize exercise programs to  
enhance benefits, promote compliance, and reduce risk of  
injury. Exercise prescription should be personalized based on an 
individual’s habits, preferences, motivation, and tolerance,  
rather than a general prescription with duration, intensity, and 
frequency.
A clinician developing an exercise regimen should not only 
consider which dose (type, intensity, duration, volume) provides 
the greatest health benefits but also review the risk profile relative 
to that dose. In other words, the clinician must balance the ben-
efits with the risks.
22 Additionally, it is imperative to assess for the 
presence of any diabetes-associated complications ( Table 29.8) 
that may limit the patient’s ability to participate in a desired  
exercise program. Of particular concern is the potential for de -
layed hypoglycemia (nocturnal hypoglycemia), a possibly fatal 
complication of the exercising individual with diabetes mellitus.
