SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
Vigorous exercise severely depletes skeletal muscle glycogen stores, 
which the liver and muscles attempt to replenish postexercise  
by extracting circulating blood glucose, resulting in profound 
hypoglycemia. This condition may occur 6 to 12 hours after  
exercise, but may develop as late as 28 hours following physical 
activity. Signs and symptoms may include seizures, cardiac ar -
rhythmias, altered mental status, altered consciousness, and death. 
The condition is especially threatening because it commonly  
develops nocturnally, during the sleeping hours. 104 Those with 
hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia risk may require medication 
dose adjustments, frequent blood glucose checks, counseling on 
carbohydrate intake prior to exercise, and hydration. Due to the 
risk of DKA in type 1 diabetes, testing for ketones is necessary 
with levels of blood glucose above 250 mg/dL. Exercise should be 
delayed if moderate to large amounts of ketones are present. 124
Oral Pharmacologic Treatment
The American College of Physicians (ACP) recommends the ad -
dition of an oral pharmacologic agent in the treatment of patients 
with type 2 diabetes when lifestyle modifications, including diet, 
exercise, and weight loss, have failed to adequately improve hyper-
glycemia. The ACP recommends initiating therapy with metfor -
min for most patients.205 Metformin, however, is contraindicated 
in patients with CHF , renal insufficiency, COPD, age older than 
80 years, and patients receiving renal transplantation given the 
possible risk of renal insufficiency.95 The ACP updated the clinical 
guidelines to include newer pharmacologic agents to treat type 2 
diabetes. The ACP recommended adding a sodium-glucose  
cotransporter-2 (SGLT-2) inhibitor (such as empagliflozin) or a 
glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) agonist (such as semaglutide)  
to metformin and lifestyle modifications in adults with type 2 
diabetes whose glycemic control is still inadequate. The SGLT-2 
inhibitor reduces the risk for all-cause mortality, major adverse 
cardiovascular events, progression of CKD, and hospitalization 
due to CHF . The GLP-1 agonist reduces the risk for all-  
cause mortality, major adverse cardiovascular events, and stroke. 
Additionally, the ACP recommends against adding a dipeptidyl 
peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor (such as sitagliptin) to metformin 
and lifestyle modification because DPP-4 inhibitor agents offer 
no benefit in reduction of all-cause mortality, major adverse  
cardiovascular events, stroke, CHF hospitalization, or CKD  
progression when compared to the SGLT-2 inhibitor or GLP-1 
agonist. Metformin and lifestyle modifications are the first line of 
treatment of type 2 diabetes in most patients. When another 
agent is needed, clinicians need to consider benefits, adverse ef -
fects, comorbidities, and associated medication costs. For those 
patients with type 2 diabetes who are also affected by CHF or 
CKD, SGLT-2 inhibitors may be favored. GLP-1 agonists may be 
more appropriate for those with increased stroke risk and when 
weight reduction is necessary. When considering hemoglobin  
A1c levels, the goal level is between 7% and 8%. When adequate 
glycemic control is achieved with metformin and either SGLT-2 
inhibitor or GLP-1 agonist, the other agents, such as sulfonyl -
ureas and long-acting insulins, should be decreased or discontin -
ued when possible to reduce the risk of hypoglycemia. 203
Diabetes Mellitus Treatment With Insulin
The ADA recommends initiation of insulin and/or other therapies 
for treatment of persistent hyperglycemia at or above 180 mg/dL, 
confirmed on two occasions for all hospitalized, noncritically ill 
patients. The goal of glucose control is 140 to 180 mg/dL.  
For those hospitalized, hyperglycemia is defined as blood glucose 
levels above 140 mg/dL. Hemoglobin A1c levels must be checked 
for all individuals who do not have an A1c level from the previous 
3 months, and a value at or above 6.5 suggests that the patient 
had diabetes prior to the hospitalization.
Blood glucose monitoring should be performed with a point-
of-care (POC) blood glucose meter before meals during the hos -
pital stay. For those on intravenous insulin therapy, more frequent 
monitoring is required, ranging from every 30 minutes to every  
2 hours. Patients with diabetes with or without automated insulin 
delivery and those using a personal continuous glucose monitor 
should continue to use these during their hospitalization.  
However, POC blood glucose measurements are still required for 
insulin dosing and hypoglycemia evaluation.
Insulin therapy, including basal, prandial, and correction insu-
lin formulations, is the preferred method of glucose management 
in most hospitalized patients with adequate nutritional intake. 
Using only a correctional insulin scale without basal insulin is 
discouraged in the hospital.14
Three types of insulin include basal, nutritional, and correc -
tional insulin. Basal insulins include formulations such as glargine 
(once daily) and neutral protamine Hagedorn (at bedtime or 
morning and bedtime). Basal insulin is required even when  
patients are not eating to account for gluconeogenesis, especially 
in those with type 1 diabetes to prevent DKA. Nutritional insulin 
covers carbohydrate intake from food and other sources. Rapid-
acting insulin, such as aspart and lispro, or short-acting insulin 
(regular) can be used for this purpose. Correctional insulin is 
needed to regulate high blood glucose to target range and utilizes 
rapid-acting or short-acting insulins.215 Dosing insulin is based on 
weight and glucose levels. Generally, half of the patient’s total 
daily dose should be given as basal or long-acting insulin, and the 
rest as premeal nutritional insulin. Although adequate glucose 
control is important to reduce and prevent complications and 
decrease overall hospital stay for patients with diabetes, current 
guidelines favor a more moderate glycemic target than in the past 
to avoid hypoglycemic episodes.137
Management of Diabetes in the Inpatient Setting
For many physiatrists, management of patients with type 2  
diabetes mellitus is likely to start in the inpatient setting, follow -
ing a diabetes-associated complication, such as stroke, coronary 
syndrome, lower limb amputation, or a prolonged hospitalization 
complicated by the comorbidity of diabetes.267
For hospitalized individuals, dysglycemia is associated with 
adverse outcomes and increased morbidity and mortality. Diabe -
tes and hyperglycemia management provided by a highly trained 
diabetes specialty team may reduce the hospital length of stay, 
prevent readmissions, and improve clinical outcomes. Diabetes 
education and a discharge plan that provides access to medication, 
supplies, and follow-up can also improve clinical outcomes.  
For the hospitalized adults, insulin is the preferred treatment op -
tion for hyperglycemia. Insulin and possibly other agents should 
be started for persistent hyperglycemia at or above 180 mg/dL. 
The recommended blood glucose goal is 140 to 180 mg/dL for 
most critically ill patients. Tighter control may be acceptable for 
those individuals without significant hypoglycemia. In noncritical 
patients, the goal of blood glucose is 100 to 180 mg/dL.
Glucose testing in the hospital should be done with POC 
blood glucose monitoring before meals. For those who are not 
eating, glucose monitoring should be done every 4 to 6 hours.

When intravenous insulin is administered, more frequent glucose 
testing is necessary. When patients use a continuous glucose 
monitor at home, they can continue using it during the hospital 
stay if clinically appropriate. However, POC glucose measure -
ments still need to be performed to assess for hypoglycemia and 
make decisions regarding insulin dosing.
If oral medications are held in the hospital and the plan is to 
restart them as outpatient, the clinician should plan to resume 
these 1 to 2 days prior to discharge.14 Patient education on diabe-
tes self-management can begin during the hospitalization. When 
an individual with diabetes is unable to complete this type of 
training due to cognitive or physical impairments, a caregiver can 
be identified and participate in education and training prior to 
discharge from the hospital. It is recommended to review and ad-
dress several areas of knowledge regarding diabetes management 
prior to discharge14:
• A follow-up with appropriate clinician to provide diabetes 
care
• Appropriate durable medical equipment, medications, and 
supplies
• Knowledge of diagnosis, glucose monitoring, and goals; 
how to address emergencies
• Recognition and treatment of hyperglycemia and hypo -
glycemia
• Nutrition
• Diabetes medication management, including insulin and 
other agents
• Proper disposal of insulin pens, syringes, needles, lancets
Treatment of Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is a diffuse, symmetric, senso -
rimotor length–dependent peripheral nerve injury that afflicts up 
to 40% of individuals with diabetes mellitus. 60 The condition re-
sults in limb pain, impaired sleep and overall quality of life, and 
may predispose to foot injuries, diabetic foot ulcers, and lower 
limb amputations. Management options include gabapentinoids 
(such as gabapentin and pregabalin), serotonin-norepinephrine 
reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) (such as duloxetine), tricyclic antide-
pressants (TCAs) (such as amitriptyline), sodium channel block -
ers (such as carbamazepine, valproic acid, lamotrigine), and 
SNRI/opioid agents (such as tramadol). However, it is important 
to note that complete resolution of symptoms is not always 
achieved. About 30% of pain reduction is considered to be  
successful in clinical trials; therefore appropriate patient counsel -
ing and management of exceptions are necessary. T reating other 
comorbid conditions, such as mood and sleep disorders, that  
can often be seen in individuals with diabetes can help achieve 
improvement in the overall quality of life.
Side effect profile and possible adverse reactions need to be 
considered when selecting from the abovementioned pharmaco -
logic agent groups. For instance, gabapentinoids can cause  
peripheral edema. TCAs can have anticholinergic side effects,  
so patients can be at risk for urinary retention, constipation, or 
orthostatic hypotension. SNRIs can cause nausea, fatigue, and 
dizziness. Valproic acid can cause teratogenic effects, hepatotoxic-
ity, and bone marrow suppression. Renal dose adjustment may  
be necessary for many of these medications. Due to similar effi -
cacy of these agents, medication choice to treat painful diabetic 
peripheral neuropathy should be guided by potential side effects, 
comorbid conditions, cost, and patient preferences. Due to lack 
of long-term efficacy and potential for severe adverse outcomes, 
including dependency and overdose, the use of opioids for  
treatment of painful diabetic neuropathy is not recommended. 
Patients may prefer topical, nontraditional, and nonpharmacologic 
treatments, which can further guide selection of treatment  
agents. T opical agents that may help with pain relief include  
capsaicin and glyceryl trinitrate spray. Ginkgo biloba is an exam -
ple of a nontraditional agent. Cognitive behavioral therapy, exer -
cise, tai chi, and mindfulness are examples of nonpharmacologic 
interventions.201
Treatment of Diabetic Gastroparesis
The physiatrist should ensure that individuals with diabetes mel -
litus who are participating in rehabilitation or exercise programs 
receive adequate nutrition to meet metabolic demands of the 
prescribed physical activity. One potential complication may 
prove to be a serious threat to the nutritional status of the patient 
with diabetes mellitus. Diabetic gastroparesis affects 5.2% of pa -
tients with type 1 and 1% with type 2 and is characterized  
by delayed gastric emptying leading to nausea, vomiting, early 
satiety, and early postprandial fullness. Patients with diabetic  
gastroparesis using insulin or insulin secretagogues who are un -
able to consume adequate carbohydrates are therefore placed  
at risk for profound hypoglycemia. Thus it is important to be 
vigilant about the diagnosis and management of this uncommon 
yet potentially deadly complication. Any patient with suspected 
gastroparesis should immediately undergo gastric emptying  
scintigraphy, which remains the standard of diagnosis. Medica -
tions that further impair gastric emptying, such as opiates and 
anticholinergics, should be discontinued. Should gastroparesis 
persist, pharmacologic agents may be used. Metoclopramide, a 
D2 dopamine receptor antagonist, remains the only FDA-  
approved medication for treatment of gastroparesis. Domperi -
done (Motilium), also a D2 dopamine antagonist, has shown 
equal efficacy to metoclopramide and may be considered for treat-
ment. Erythromycin, a motilin-stimulating antibiotic, has been 
proven effective in the management of diabetic gastroparesis when 
administered intravenously. Symptomatic management of nausea 
and vomiting with phenothiazines, promethazine, or ondansetron 
may provide additional relief. At present, intrapyloric botulinum 
toxin injection has shown no symptomatic improvement and is 
therefore not recommended. Gastric electrical stimulation, by 
contrast, is associated with symptomatic benefits. For patients 
failing conservative treatment, surgical management such as  
venting gastrostomy, gastrojejunostomy, pyloroplasty, and gas -
trectomy may be considered.44
Summary
With an aging population, there is an increasing proportion of 
individuals living with chronic medical conditions, including 
pulmonary pathology, organ failure requiring transplantation, 
and diabetes mellitus with multiple diabetic complications. 
Given the complexity of these conditions, management cannot 
be compartmentalized and handled by only one medical special -
ist. Additionally, chronic medical diseases often directly or indi -
rectly lead to impairments in patient function; therefore it is 
imperative for the physiatrist to become knowledgeable and  
involved in the management of the patient with chronic medical 
conditions.
